proposals.title: EcoFlex: An Ecosystem of Task-Specialized Models for High-Throughput Inference to Close the Human-Machine Performance Gap
proposals.motivation: Despite significant advances, a substantial performance gap persists between state-of-the-art models and human-level performance on complex, domain-specific tasks (e.g., 51.0% F1 vs. 86.8% human). Closing this gap is critical for deploying reliable AI assistants in medicine, law, and science. Current monolithic large language models (LLMs) are computationally expensive and may be suboptimal for specialized domains where nuanced understanding is required.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a curated ecosystem of smaller, domain-adapted models, generated and orchestrated via high-throughput inference, can achieve performance significantly closer to human experts on targeted tasks than a single general-purpose LLM.
proposals.method: We will leverage FlexGen's high-throughput generation framework to efficiently train and infer with an ensemble of task-specialized models. Using a generator-inference paradigm, we will first generate a diverse set of candidate model architectures and training curricula tailored to sub-domains of the target task. These models will then be orchestrated within an inference ecosystem that uses learned routing and consensus mechanisms to combine their predictions, drawing inspiration from robust watermarking techniques for model attribution and integrity.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a scalable framework for building high-performance, cost-effective model ecosystems for specialized domains. It will provide empirical evidence on the trade-offs between model specialization, ensemble diversity, and inference efficiency, moving beyond the one-model-fits-all paradigm.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in synthesizing high-throughput inference (FlexGen) with an ecosystem approach to model specialization, moving from a single model to a coordinated suite. This contrasts with merely scaling up a single model or using simple ensembling of homogeneous models.

proposals.title: Syntax-Informed Question Answering via Latent Tree Integration and High-Throughput Inference
proposals.motivation: Despite the success of end-to-end neural models on QA benchmarks, they often fail on questions requiring deep syntactic understanding, such as those involving negation, long-range dependencies, or complex coreference. Explicit syntactic structures (dependency and constituency trees) have proven valuable for analysis but are underutilized as direct features for improving model robustness and generalization in comprehension tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating explicit syntactic parses as a latent, structured component within a transformer-based QA model will improve its performance on linguistically challenging questions without sacrificing efficiency, and this syntactic guidance can be distilled for high-throughput inference.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage method leveraging Percy Liang's recent work on efficient systems. First, we develop a 'Syntax-Informed Transformer' where predicted dependency and constituency trees are encoded via graph attention networks and softly gated into the intermediate layers of a model like T5 or a decoder-only LLM. Second, drawing from FlexGen and high-throughput inference techniques, we design a knowledge distillation pipeline where the syntax-augmented teacher model trains a smaller, syntax-free student model to internalize the structural biases, enabling efficient deployment.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel architecture that fuses explicit syntactic knowledge with pre-trained language models for QA, 2) a systematic evaluation demonstrating gains on syntactic challenge sets (e.g., SQuAD-hard, BoolQ), and 3) a practical distillation framework demonstrating how such structural knowledge can be incorporated into models suitable for high-throughput generative inference.
proposals.novelty: While syntax has been used for analysis or as input features in older NLP models, its integration as a latent, trainable signal within modern, large-scale QA models and its subsequent distillation for efficient inference is an unexplored synthesis, moving beyond using syntax solely as a diagnostic tool.

proposals.title: Dynamic Influence Functions for Online Language Model Adaptation
proposals.motivation: Current methods for quantifying data influence (e.g., influence functions) assume a static, fixed training set, which is unrealistic for models that learn continuously from evolving data streams like user feedback or web crawls. This static assumption limits our ability to audit, debug, and responsibly update models in real-world deployment. Understanding how past data points dynamically shape a model's current behavior in an online setting is a critical open problem for trustworthy AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by extending influence function theory to online convex optimization frameworks, we can efficiently and accurately track the evolving influence of training data points on model predictions as the training dataset grows and changes.
proposals.method: We will develop an algorithmic framework, Dynamic Influence Tracking (DIT), building on pre-2026 online learning theory (e.g., follow-the-regularized-leader) and efficient influence estimation techniques. DIT will maintain a running approximation of the inverse Hessian-vector product, updated incrementally as new data arrives, inspired by the high-throughput inference optimizations in FlexGen. We will validate DIT on a controlled online learning ecosystem, where a language model is continuously fine-tuned on a stream of text data with known properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first principled method for computing data influence in non-stationary training environments. It will contribute new tools for monitoring and explaining the behavior of continually learning language models, directly impacting research in model transparency, data attribution, and safe online adaptation.
proposals.novelty: While influence functions are well-studied for static models, applying and scaling them to a streaming, non-i.i.d. data setting with efficient incremental updates is a novel and technically challenging direction not addressed by prior work.

proposals.title: The Synthetic Gap: A Systematic Test of Scaling Law Transfer from GINC to Natural and Multimodal Data
proposals.motivation: Scaling laws derived from synthetic datasets like GINC (Generative In-Context) have become foundational for predicting model performance. However, it is unclear if these laws, which rely on controlled, simplified data, fully capture the complexities of natural language and multimodal distributions. Validating their transferability is critical for reliable model scaling and efficient resource allocation in real-world applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that scaling laws derived from GINC data will systematically diverge when models are trained and evaluated on natural language corpora and complex multimodal data, due to unmodeled statistical and structural phenomena.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled scaling study using the FlexGen high-throughput inference framework to efficiently train a series of transformer models (from 10M to 1B parameters) on three data regimes: 1) the original GINC synthetic data, 2) a curated natural language corpus (e.g., C4) filtered to match GINC's token distribution, and 3) a multimodal dataset (e.g., image-caption pairs from COCO/LAION) tokenized into a sequential format. We will measure scaling laws for cross-entropy loss and in-context learning performance on held-out natural and synthetic tasks, using inference-time algorithms to probe model capabilities.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic evidence quantifying the 'synthetic gap' for neural scaling laws. It will yield practical guidance on the limits of synthetic data for predicting real-world model performance and may inspire new, more robust scaling methodologies for complex domains.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses synthetic data to *discover* scaling laws, this proposal directly tests their *external validity* across a fidelity gradient from synthetic tokens to natural and multimodal data, a crucial but understudied question.

proposals.title: Meta-In-Context: A Theoretical Framework for In-Context Learning as Implicit Gradient-Based Meta-Inference
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) perform in-context learning (ICL) by adapting to tasks presented in a prompt without weight updates, but its theoretical underpinnings remain poorly understood. Drawing a formal analogy between ICL and gradient-based meta-learning could provide rigorous tools to analyze and improve this emergent capability, which is central to the flexible, high-throughput deployment of models studied in Liang's lab.
proposals.hypothesis: In-context learning in autoregressive transformers can be formally characterized as performing an implicit, few-step gradient-based meta-optimization process during the forward pass, and metrics from meta-learning theory can predict ICL performance and failure modes.
proposals.method: We will adapt the optimization dynamics analysis from MAML and related meta-learning algorithms to the forward pass of a transformer. Using synthetic, controlled tasks, we will measure alignment between the model's implicit adaptation (via attention) and explicit gradient steps on a task loss defined by the in-context examples. We will extend tools from FlexGen's inference-time analysis to track these 'meta-gradients' and develop a novel metric, In-Context Meta-Gradient Alignment (ICMGA), to quantify the efficiency of ICL across domains.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel theoretical lens for ICL, connecting it to established meta-learning literature. It will yield practical diagnostics for predicting when ICL will succeed or fail for a given task and model, guiding more efficient prompt construction and model evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has suggested superficial parallels, this proposal provides a concrete, testable technical framework to formally bridge ICL and meta-learning optimization theory, moving beyond analogy to measurable alignment.

proposals.title: The Emergence Threshold: Scaling Laws for Latent Concept Inference in Minimal Transformers
proposals.motivation: In-context learning (ICL) is a remarkable emergent ability of large language models, but its origins remain poorly understood. Determining the minimal computational and data requirements for ICL to arise is a fundamental scientific question with practical implications for efficient model design. This work seeks to establish scaling laws for the emergence of ICL as a function of model and dataset size, moving beyond mere performance curves to probe the underlying capability for latent concept inference.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that there exists a sharp, predictable threshold in the scaling of model parameters and training tokens beyond which transformers transition from memorization to robust in-context learning of latent task concepts from demonstrations.
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic scaling study, training a population of small Transformer models (1M to 100M parameters) on controlled, procedurally generated text datasets of varying sizes and concept complexities. Inspired by the high-throughput methods of FlexGen, we will automate this training and evaluation ecosystem. We will develop a diagnostic benchmark of synthetic in-context tasks (e.g., word manipulation, simple reasoning) and use inference-time analysis to measure when models begin to infer and apply latent task rules from demonstrations, rather than relying on surface-level patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first detailed phase diagram for the emergence of in-context learning, identifying critical scaling exponents for model and data. It will contribute a rigorous, minimal testbed for studying mechanistic interpretability of emergent behaviors. The findings will inform the design of more efficient, capable small-scale models by pinpointing the necessary conditions for meta-learning.
proposals.novelty: While scaling laws for loss exist, we propose scaling laws specifically for the emergence of a *mechanistic capability*—latent concept inference. Our approach uniquely combines high-throughput experimental design with a focus on the minimal viable model, a significant downscaling from the billion-parameter regimes typically studied for ICL.

proposals.title: Adaptive Token Generation: Learning Dynamic Embedding Mappings During Inference
proposals.motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) map latent representations to tokens via a fixed, static output embedding matrix. This rigidity limits a model's ability to adapt its generative vocabulary to specific contexts, domains, or user intents during inference, potentially harming performance on specialized tasks or creative generation. An adaptive mapping could improve efficiency and quality by focusing the output space on relevant tokens.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that making the mapping from latents to tokens contextually adaptive and learnable during the generation process will improve the performance, efficiency, and controllability of LLMs compared to models with a fixed output layer.
proposals.method: We will develop a lightweight, plug-in module that dynamically modulates the output embedding matrix based on the ongoing generation context. Inspired by mixture-of-experts and FlexGen's high-throughput design, our method will use a learned router to select sparse, context-dependent combinations of 'embedding experts' (subsets of the full vocabulary) to form a transient output layer for each generation step. This will be integrated and trained using next-token prediction, with evaluation on domain-specific and creative generation tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new method for adaptive vocabulary selection during LLM inference, potentially increasing throughput by reducing softmax computation over a full vocabulary. It will also provide empirical evidence on how dynamic output spaces affect generative performance and controllability, extending research on efficient and specialized generation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work like adaptive softmax reduces fixed vocabulary size for efficiency, our approach makes the mapping itself a context-dependent, learned function *during generation*, a departure from static embeddings. This integrates inference-time adaptation directly into the token-generation mechanism, which is largely unexplored.

proposals.title: Diffusion-LM++: Scaling Diffusion-Based Fine-Tuning to Billion-Parameter Language Models
proposals.motivation: Diffusion-LM (Li et al., 2022) introduced a promising, continuous diffusion-based method for fine-grained, latent-space control of language generation. However, its experiments were limited to models under 1B parameters (e.g., GPT-2), leaving its scalability to modern, high-performance LLMs (e.g., Llama 2, GPT-3) an open question. This research is critical to determine if diffusion-based fine-tuning can retain its precise controllability advantages while leveraging the superior base knowledge and fluency of state-of-the-art large models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Diffusion-LM architecture can be successfully adapted to pre-trained LLMs with 7B+ parameters, and that this scaled approach will outperform standard fine-tuning methods (e.g., LoRA) on fine-grained control tasks by enabling smoother, more stable latent-space optimization.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Diffusion-LM framework to operate on the latent representations of a frozen, large pre-trained decoder (e.g., Llama 2 7B). Key technical challenges include designing memory-efficient diffusion training over high-dimensional latents and integrating it with high-throughput inference techniques like FlexGen. We will employ a two-stage process: first training an encoder to map discrete tokens to continuous latents, then training a diffusion model over these latents, using gradient checkpointing and parameter-efficient adapters to manage GPU memory.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical evidence on the scalability and practical efficacy of diffusion models for controlling large, off-the-shelf language models. It will deliver a new fine-tuning paradigm for precise attribute control and could establish diffusion as a viable alternative to autoregressive fine-tuning for specific, constrained generation tasks in the large-model regime.
proposals.novelty: While diffusion for language is known, applying it to fine-tune models at the 7B+ scale is unexplored. The non-obvious challenge is efficiently training a diffusion process in the latent space of a massive, frozen model without catastrophic forgetting or prohibitive memory costs.

proposals.title: Task and Architecture Agnosticism of the 'Lost-in-the-Middle' Phenomenon in Long-Context Language Models
proposals.motivation: Recent work (Liu et al., 2023) identified the 'lost-in-the-middle' phenomenon, where LLMs fail to utilize relevant information located in the middle of long contexts, primarily in multi-document QA. This is a critical bottleneck for deploying LLMs in real-world applications requiring long-context understanding, such as legal document review or long-form summarization. However, its generality across diverse task formulations and model architectures remains unexplored, limiting our understanding of its root cause.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the 'lost-in-the-middle' effect is a general failure mode of current Transformer-based LLMs, persisting across diverse architectures (e.g., encoder-decoder vs. decoder-only) and task types (e.g., summarization, multi-hop reasoning), but that its severity is modulated by model family, training data, and specific attention mechanisms.
proposals.method: We will systematically benchmark the phenomenon using a controlled experimental framework. We will evaluate models from distinct families (e.g., GPT-4, Claude, Llama 2, T5) on a suite of long-context tasks including summarization (e.g., GovReport), multi-document QA, and synthetic needle-in-a-haystack retrieval. We will employ the analysis methods from the original study, measuring performance as a function of relevant information position, and extend them with layer-wise attention head analysis. High-throughput evaluation will be facilitated by adapting efficient inference techniques like FlexGen.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive map of the 'lost-in-the-middle' effect's scope, identifying which model architectures and tasks are most susceptible. It will deliver actionable insights for practitioners selecting models for long-context applications and for researchers developing more robust long-context architectures and training methods.
proposals.novelty: While the initial finding was task- and model-specific, our systematic investigation across the axes of task and architecture is novel. It moves from documenting a single observation to establishing a generalized empirical law about LLM limitations, distinguishing between core architectural weaknesses and task-specific failures.

proposals.title: Does Fine-Tuning Teach Models to Listen? Evaluating Instruction Tuning for Robust Long-Context Utilization
proposals.motivation: While large language models (LLMs) now support context windows of 100K+ tokens, their ability to robustly use information distributed across these contexts remains unclear. This is a critical bottleneck for real-world applications like legal document analysis, long-form QA, and codebase reasoning, where performance depends on synthesizing scattered details. Current evaluations often focus on simple retrieval tasks, failing to assess the complex reasoning required for genuine long-context use.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that instruction tuning, particularly on tasks requiring cross-context synthesis and reasoning, will significantly improve a model's robust utilization of long-context information compared to base pre-trained models, and that this improvement is not merely due to task-specific memorization but to a generalized ability to attend to and integrate distant information.
proposals.method: We will construct a controlled benchmark, 'LongContextEval', featuring tasks where necessary information is sparsely distributed and requires reasoning (e.g., multi-hop QA, narrative consistency checks, and summarization with latent constraints). Using base models like LLaMA-2 (pre-2026), we will perform systematic fine-tuning ablations: (1) standard instruction tuning (e.g., on Alpaca mixtures), (2) targeted long-context task tuning on our benchmark, and (3) a control with no fine-tuning. Evaluation will use established metrics and novel 'needle-in-a-haystack' diagnostic probes inspired by recent inference efficiency work (e.g., FlexGen), measuring accuracy as a function of information position and dependency distance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first controlled evidence quantifying how fine-tuning shapes long-context reasoning, moving beyond simple retrieval. It will yield a public diagnostic benchmark for long-context utilization and offer practical guidance on whether and how to fine-tune models for long-context applications.
proposals.novelty: Prior work has scaled context length or evaluated retrieval, but has not isolated the effect of fine-tuning on the *robust reasoning* capabilities within long contexts. Our controlled ablation study directly tests if instruction tuning teaches models to 'listen' across extended text, a non-obvious outcome given that pre-training may already provide the necessary architectural capacity.

proposals.title: Adversarial Context Generation for Stress-Testing Long-Context Reasoning in Language Models
proposals.motivation: As language models scale to handle longer contexts (e.g., 128K+ tokens), their ability to reliably retrieve and reason over information placed in the middle of these contexts remains poorly understood and difficult to evaluate systematically. Current benchmarks rely on static, human-written documents, which may not expose worst-case failure modes critical for deploying models in high-stakes, long-form applications like legal analysis or scientific literature review.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an adversarial generation process can efficiently synthesize long-context documents that systematically induce information loss in a target model's mid-context recall, and that these generated contexts will reveal more severe and generalizable model weaknesses than existing static benchmarks.
proposals.method: We propose an inverse generation framework. Given a target LLM (e.g., LLaMA, GPT-4), we will use a smaller, controllable model (fine-tuned for instruction following) as a generator, optimized via a reinforcement learning loop. The reward signal will be based on the target model's failure to correctly answer factual queries about information strategically placed in the middle of the generated long document. We will build upon inference optimization techniques like FlexGen to make the repeated, iterative evaluation of long contexts computationally feasible.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield: 1) a scalable, automated method for creating adversarial long-context evaluation suites, 2) a diagnostic benchmark revealing specific architectural or attention-based vulnerabilities in long-context models, and 3) open-sourced tools and a dataset of generated adversarial contexts to spur further research into robust long-context handling.
proposals.novelty: While prior work creates adversarial examples by perturbing inputs, we propose *generating* entirely novel, coherent long-form contexts optimized to be adversarial, moving beyond simple perturbations. This inverts the typical model evaluation paradigm from passive testing to active, adaptive probing.

proposals.title: Distributionally Robust Fine-tuning for Domain Generalization in Language and Vision-Language Models
proposals.motivation: Distributionally Robust Optimization (DRO) coupled with regularization has shown promise for improving out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization in controlled settings. However, its efficacy across diverse, real-world tasks and modern, large-scale model architectures remains underexplored, limiting its practical adoption for robust AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a regularization-coupled DRO framework, when applied to a broad spectrum of tasks and model families, will yield consistent and significant improvements in OOD performance compared to standard empirical risk minimization, without catastrophic trade-offs on in-distribution accuracy.
proposals.method: We will extend the regularization-coupled DRO framework (e.g., combining DRO with gradient penalty or spectral regularization) to a curated suite of tasks including text classification, question answering, and image captioning. We will test it on diverse architectures: (1) pure language models (e.g., T5 variants), (2) autoregressive vision-language models (inspired by Anticipatory Music Transformer's causal modeling), and (3) encoder-decoder models. Evaluation will use established OOD benchmarks (e.g., WILDS, DomainBed) and high-throughput inference techniques (inspired by FlexGen) for efficient assessment.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a comprehensive empirical study of DRO's scalability and generalizability, establishing clear guidelines for its application. It will deliver robustly fine-tuned checkpoints for multiple model families, advancing the field towards more reliable and deployable models.
proposals.novelty: While DRO has been studied, its systematic integration with modern regularization techniques and evaluation across the full spectrum from language to vision-language tasks using contemporary large models is novel. The architectural insights from causal generative models (like the Anticipatory Music Transformer) applied to robustness are also unexplored.

proposals.title: FLEXGEN-FLUENT: A Latent Fusion Transformer for Coherent Neural Generation from Retrieved Phrases
proposals.motivation: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems often struggle with the fluency of their final output, as the stitching of retrieved text fragments with generated content can produce awkward or incoherent text. This fluency gap limits the practical utility of RAG in high-stakes domains like report writing or dialogue, where naturalness is critical. Improving this final generation step is a key challenge for making retrieval-based models more performant and reliable.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a neural generation model trained to explicitly reason over and fuse a latent representation of retrieved phrases with the model's own contextual planning will produce more fluent and coherent final text than standard autoregressive generation conditioned on raw retrieved text.
proposals.method: We will develop a Latent Fusion Transformer (LFT) architecture that extends the anticipatory decoding principles of the Anticipatory Music Transformer to the text domain. The model will use a dual-encoder to project retrieved text spans and the model's own 'deleted content' (planned future tokens) into a shared latent space. A novel cross-attention 'fusion layer' will integrate these signals before the final generation step. We will train and evaluate the model using the FLEXGEN high-throughput inference framework on a curated mixture of tasks requiring precise phrase integration, such as long-form QA and document continuation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, trainable model for the final fusion step in RAG pipelines, directly addressing the fluency bottleneck. It will contribute a publicly available benchmark for evaluating generation coherence in retrieval-augmented settings and demonstrate how high-throughput inference systems like FLEXGEN can be leveraged for training specialized fusion models.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard sequence-to-sequence models that treat retrieved text as part of the input context, our method explicitly models the fusion of retrieved and generated content in a learned latent space, an approach not yet explored for general text generation. It uniquely combines concepts from anticipatory generation, high-throughput inference, and latent representation learning for this specific problem.

proposals.title: FlexGen-LLM: High-Throughput Generative Inference for Low-Resource Language Sentiment Analysis
proposals.motivation: Sentiment analysis tools are critical for global NLP applications, yet they are predominantly available for high-resource languages. For low-resource languages, the scarcity of large labeled datasets and comprehensive sentiment lexicons creates a significant performance and accessibility gap. This limits equitable access to language technology and hinders cross-lingual understanding in a multilingual world.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by adapting high-throughput, memory-efficient inference methods like FlexGen to a novel pipeline combining few-shot generation and lexicon induction, we can achieve robust sentiment analysis performance for low-resource languages without requiring large labeled corpora.
proposals.method: We will adapt the FlexGen inference engine to efficiently run a large, multilingual LLM (e.g., BLOOM or XGLM) for a two-stage task. First, we use the model for few-shot generation to create silver-standard sentiment annotations from minimal seed examples. Second, we employ a constrained generation or inference-time algorithm, inspired by anticipatory decoding techniques, to iteratively expand and refine a language-specific sentiment lexicon from model outputs and minimal external linguistic cues, validating against small human-labeled sets.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a practical, efficient framework for building sentiment analysis systems for low-resource languages, reducing dependency on large labeled data. It will also advance the methodology for adapting high-throughput inference systems to data-scarce, knowledge-intensive NLP tasks beyond English.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in repurposing high-throughput LLM inference (FlexGen) not just for speed, but as a core, efficient engine for iterative lexicon building and annotation in low-resource settings, moving beyond direct fine-tuning or static prompting approaches.

proposals.title: Generative Data Augmentation at Scale: Improving Sentiment Classification Robustness via High-Throughput LLM Inference
proposals.motivation: High-performance sentiment classification models require large, diverse, and high-quality training datasets, which are expensive and time-consuming to curate manually. Pre-2026, data augmentation techniques for NLP often rely on simple lexical substitutions or back-translation, which can lack semantic diversity and coherence. This project addresses the need for scalable, semantically rich data generation to improve model robustness and performance across domains.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using a high-throughput inference system to generate large-scale, label-conditioned text from a powerful base language model will serve as an effective data augmentation tool, significantly improving the accuracy and domain robustness of smaller, downstream sentiment classifiers compared to existing augmentation methods.
proposals.method: We will leverage FlexGen, a high-throughput generative inference framework, to efficiently generate massive volumes of synthetic, sentiment-labeled text (positive/negative) from a model like GPT-3 or T5. This generation will be conditioned on prompts and seeds from existing datasets (e.g., SST-2). We will then fine-tune a BERT-family model on a mixture of original and augmented data. The core evaluation will compare the performance of models trained with our augmented data against baselines using back-translation or EDA, focusing on in-domain accuracy and out-of-domain generalization (e.g., to movie reviews, product reviews, social media).
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a practical pipeline for using high-throughput LLM inference as a general-purpose data augmentation engine for specific NLP tasks. It will provide empirical evidence on the scaling laws of synthetic data quality and quantity for sentiment analysis. The resulting models and generation framework will be released to advance research in efficient data-centric AI.
proposals.novelty: While LLMs have been used for text generation, the novel integration lies in applying high-throughput, cost-effective inference systems (FlexGen) specifically for large-scale, task-targeted data augmentation, moving beyond one-off generation or prompt engineering to systematic data creation. This approach treats 'scale' itself—in terms of generated data volume and inference efficiency—as the primary augmentation tool.

proposals.title: Probing for Selectivity: A Control Task Benchmark for Contemporary Representation Models
proposals.motivation: Probing has become a standard method for interpreting the linguistic knowledge encoded in models like BERT and GPT. However, recent work (e.g., Hewitt and Liang, 2019) highlights that probes can learn tasks rather than simply reading out representations, raising concerns about selectivity. The field lacks a systematic, comparative evaluation of probe selectivity across the diverse landscape of pre-trained representation models (encoder-only, decoder-only, encoder-decoder) using a unified control task framework.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the selectivity of linear probes, measured by the performance gap between real and control tasks, varies significantly across model architectures (e.g., BERT vs. GPT) and layers, and is influenced by factors like model size and pre-training objective.
proposals.method: We will adapt the control task methodology to evaluate a suite of models including BERT, RoBERTa, GPT-2, and T5. For each linguistic property (e.g., syntactic depth, semantic role), we will train linear classifiers on the model's frozen representations using both the real task and a randomized control task. We will compute selectivity scores and analyze trends across layers and model families. The evaluation will be integrated into an open-source probing ecosystem, extending frameworks like `jiant` or `transformers` to ensure reproducibility and high-throughput inference, potentially leveraging efficient inference techniques like those in FlexGen for larger models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive benchmark of probe selectivity for major contemporary representation models, yielding practical guidance for interpretability research. We will release a standardized suite of control tasks and analysis tools to help the community build more reliable probes, advancing rigorous model interpretation.
proposals.novelty: While control tasks are established, their systematic application to compare selectivity across the full spectrum of modern transformer-based architectures (encoders, decoders, and encoder-decoders) is novel. This moves beyond analyzing a single model family to uncover architectural and training determinants of probe reliability.

proposals.title: Probing for Latent World Models: A Control-Task Framework for Isolating Non-Linguistic Structure in LLM Representations
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit impressive performance on tasks requiring world knowledge and reasoning, but it remains unclear whether their internal representations encode structured, non-linguistic models of the world or merely sophisticated linguistic correlations. Disentangling this is crucial for understanding model capabilities, limitations, and potential for grounding. Pre-2026, probing studies often conflate linguistic and factual knowledge, lacking rigorous controls.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that LLM representations encode disentangleable, structured representations of world knowledge (e.g., physical, temporal, or social relations) that are distinct from purely linguistic form and can be selectively activated via controlled inference tasks.
proposals.method: We will design a suite of *control tasks* that require identical linguistic surface form but differ in underlying non-linguistic structure (e.g., "The cup is on the table" vs. "The book is on the table," paired with true/false physical arrangement queries). Leveraging methods from Percy Liang's recent work, we will use efficient, high-throughput probing via FlexGen to perform targeted inference on model activations. We will apply causal mediation analysis to isolate specific model components responsible for representing the non-linguistic variables, extending beyond standard linear probing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, controlled framework for determining what kinds of world knowledge are encoded in LLMs and how they are structurally represented. It will contribute new diagnostic benchmarks and analysis techniques to the field of model interpretability, moving beyond correlational probes to causal tests of representation content.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit decoupling of linguistic form from non-linguistic content through carefully matched control tasks, combined with high-throughput causal analysis methods adapted from efficient inference research, a approach not yet standardized in pre-2026 probing literature.

proposals.title: Dual-Training Language Models with Probing-Based Auxiliary Objectives for Improved Representational Control
proposals.motivation: Pre-trained language models develop rich internal representations, but controlling or steering these representations for specific downstream tasks or properties remains a challenge. Current methods often rely on post-hoc fine-tuning or prompting, which can be inefficient or fail to fundamentally alter the model's latent space. A method to directly shape representations during training using explicit, interpretable signals would enable more controllable and efficient models.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating the performance of lightweight, selective linear probes—trained to predict specific linguistic or semantic properties from intermediate model activations—as an auxiliary training signal will improve the base model's representations, making them more conducive to those targeted properties without sacrificing general language modeling performance.
proposals.method: We will extend the training objective of a causal language model (e.g., a GPT-style decoder) with an auxiliary loss term derived from a suite of linear probes. During training, we will periodically extract activations from chosen model layers, train simple probes on small, curated datasets for properties like syntactic structure, factual knowledge, or sentiment, and backpropagate the probe loss gradients into the base model parameters. This creates a dual-training loop where the model is optimized for both next-token prediction and for developing linearly decodable features. We will experiment with scheduling the auxiliary loss and selecting probe sets to avoid interference.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel training paradigm for integrating interpretability tools (probes) directly into model optimization. It will provide empirical evidence on whether and how probe-guided training can shape representations, potentially leading to models with more steerable internal states. The method could reduce the need for extensive task-specific fine-tuning.
proposals.novelty: While probes are widely used for *analysis*, using their performance as a *training signal* to guide the primary model's learning is a non-obvious inversion of their typical role. This differs from multi-task learning as the auxiliary tasks are defined by the very act of probing the model's own intermediate representations.

proposals.title: Task-Aware Head Initialization for Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning
proposals.motivation: Standard fine-tuning of large language models (LLMs) often initializes the final output layer (head) randomly or keeps it fixed, which can be suboptimal for data-scarce tasks. This practice ignores the potential of leveraging pre-existing, high-quality head parameters from smaller models already specialized for the target task. Given the push for high-throughput and efficient inference (e.g., FlexGen), improving sample efficiency and final performance of fine-tuning is crucial.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that initializing the head of a large foundation model with parameters pre-trained on the target task, and then freezing it during fine-tuning, will lead to faster convergence, higher final performance, and better parameter efficiency compared to standard random initialization or full fine-tuning.
proposals.method: We will adapt the parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) ecosystem, specifically LoRA or prefix-tuning, applied to a frozen base LLM (e.g., LLaMA). Crucially, we will replace the model's randomly initialized classification/decoding head with a head pre-trained on the target task using a smaller, task-specific model (e.g., a BERT-family model for GLUE). This head remains frozen. We will benchmark performance on NLP tasks (GLUE, SuperGLUE) against baselines of full fine-tuning and PEFT with a random head, measuring accuracy, convergence speed, and robustness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled method for injecting prior task knowledge into LLM fine-tuning, potentially establishing a new best practice for low-resource adaptation. It contributes to the field of efficient inference by reducing the trainable parameter count and improving sample efficiency, directly aligning with high-throughput generation goals.
proposals.novelty: While PEFT methods freeze the base model, the systematic freezing and pre-initialization of the head using a distinct, pre-trained model is novel. It challenges the default treatment of the head as a disposable, randomly initialized component.

proposals.title: The Few-Shot Fragility Frontier: Quantifying the In-Distribution vs. Out-of-Distribution Trade-off in Low-Data Fine-Tuning
proposals.motivation: Fine-tuning large language models (LLMs) on small, task-specific datasets is a cornerstone of modern NLP, enabling adaptation to specialized domains. However, pre-2026 research on the ID-OOD trade-off has primarily focused on high-data regimes, leaving a critical gap: we lack a principled understanding of how this fundamental tension manifests and can be managed when only a handful of examples are available. This is a pressing issue for real-world deployment where labeled data is scarce and models must generalize robustly beyond their fine-tuning distribution.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in few-shot and one-shot fine-tuning regimes, the ID-OOD performance trade-off is governed by a distinct, quantifiable 'fragility frontier,' where gains on in-distribution examples come at a disproportionately high cost to out-of-distribution robustness compared to high-data regimes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled empirical study using a suite of text classification and generation tasks, systematically varying the number of fine-tuning examples (from one-shot to 100-shot). We will leverage and extend methods from Liang's prior work: using the 'ecosystem' framework to define task distributions and measure OOD performance, and employing efficient inference techniques like those in FlexGen to enable high-throughput evaluation across thousands of model configurations. Our core analysis will model the ID-OOD Pareto frontier as a function of data quantity, fine-tuning strategy (e.g., full-parameter vs. parameter-efficient), and model scale.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic characterization of the ID-OOD trade-off in low-data regimes, yielding practical guidelines for safe and effective few-shot adaptation. We will release a benchmark and diagnostic toolkit for evaluating fine-tuning fragility, contributing directly to the field's understanding of robust transfer learning.
proposals.novelty: While the ID-OOD trade-off is known, its systematic study under severe data constraints—and the discovery of a quantifiable 'fragility frontier'—is novel. This connects the robustness literature with the practical realities of few-shot learning, an underexplored intersection.

proposals.title: Structured Knowledge Infusion: Improving LM Pretraining with Graph-Guided Objectives
proposals.motivation: Current language model (LM) pretraining relies on next-token prediction, which struggles to capture structured, relational knowledge efficiently. While methods like QA-GNN use LMs to assist knowledge graph (KG) reasoning, this leaves the LM's fundamental knowledge acquisition untouched. This project inverts the assistance, using the explicit reasoning of KGs to create better, more structured pretraining objectives for LMs, addressing a core limitation in how they learn.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training objectives derived from the structured reasoning paths of a model like QA-GNN can inject more robust and relational knowledge into an LM during pretraining, leading to improved performance on downstream tasks requiring factual and multi-hop reasoning.
proposals.method: We will adapt the QA-GNN framework to generate reasoning chains over a large-scale KG (e.g., Wikidata) for a diverse corpus of text. These chains, representing structured inference steps, will be used to create novel pretraining objectives beyond masked language modeling. Specifically, we will design a 'graph-infused' objective where the LM must predict masked entities or relations conditioned on the surrounding KG subgraph, and a 'path-ranking' objective where it must order plausible reasoning paths. We will integrate these objectives into a high-throughput training pipeline, leveraging methods like FlexGen for efficient large-scale experimentation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel methodology for integrating structured knowledge into LM pretraining via graph-derived objectives, 2) a new pretrained model demonstrating improved performance on knowledge-intensive and reasoning benchmarks, and 3) an analysis of how different structured objectives affect the learned representations of LMs.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the dominant paradigm of using LMs to assist KG reasoning (as in QA-GNN) by using KG reasoning to fundamentally improve LM pretraining, creating a novel, bidirectional synergy. The approach moves beyond simple entity linking or retrieval-augmentation to modify the core pretraining objective itself using graph structure.

proposals.title: Beyond Linear Composition: Diagnosing and Modeling Non-Linear Concept Interactions in Language Models
proposals.motivation: Modern interpretability methods often assume that model predictions can be decomposed into a linear combination of independent, human-understandable concepts. This assumption underpins popular techniques like concept activation vectors (CAVs) and linear probes. However, the complex, non-linear nature of neural networks suggests this linearity and independence may be a flawed simplification, limiting our ability to faithfully explain model behavior, especially in high-stakes domains.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that critical semantic concepts within language models are neither independent nor combined linearly for final predictions, and that explicitly modeling their non-linear interactions is essential for accurate mechanistic interpretation and controlled generation.
proposals.method: We will develop a suite of diagnostic tasks to quantify concept non-linearity and interdependence, extending the 'inference' and 'tasks' focus of Liang's prior work. Building on architectures like the Anticipatory Music Transformer, which models hierarchical dependencies, we will design a novel 'Concept Interaction Transformer' that explicitly parameterizes interactions between concept representations. We will use efficient 'generation' and 'inference' frameworks like FlexGen to train and probe these models at scale, measuring the performance gap between linear and non-linear concept composition models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) empirical evidence challenging the linear independence assumption in interpretability, 2) a new diagnostic benchmark for concept interactions, and 3) a modeling framework that enables more faithful explanations and fine-grained control over concept combinations in text generation, advancing the 'ecosystem' of trustworthy AI tools.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identifies individual concepts, we directly test and model their *interactions*. The novelty lies in moving from static concept extraction to dynamic, non-linear interaction modeling, integrating interpretability research with controlled generation architectures in a novel way.

proposals.title: FlexGen-Interactive: High-Throughput Online Learning from Human Concept Corrections
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) are trained on static datasets, making it difficult to correct systematic errors or update their conceptual understanding post-deployment. Pre-2026, methods for incorporating real-time human feedback, like RLHF, are often complex, offline, and focus on preference alignment rather than direct factual or conceptual correction. An efficient, interactive training loop that ingests human corrections on-the-fly could enable more adaptable and accurate models.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a high-throughput, online training mechanism that directly incorporates human corrections of predicted concepts into the forward pass will lead to more sample-efficient correction of model errors and improved performance on downstream tasks requiring precise conceptual understanding.
proposals.method: We will extend the FlexGen high-throughput inference framework to support lightweight, online fine-tuning. The system will interleave inference and training: during interactive use, a human annotator will flag and correct erroneous model outputs (e.g., "predicted concept A is wrong, correct concept is B"). These (input, corrected_output) pairs will be batched and used to compute a gradient via a small, cached auxiliary network or adapter attached to the main LLM. Leveraging FlexGen's efficient attention and offloading, we will perform a single, constrained gradient step on this batch before resuming inference, ensuring low latency. The correction data will also be logged for periodic larger-scale retraining.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel system architecture merging high-throughput inference with online learning, extending the FlexGen ecosystem; 2) an empirical demonstration of how direct conceptual feedback improves model accuracy more efficiently than offline feedback methods; and 3) a public framework for interactive model correction.
proposals.novelty: While interactive learning exists in robotics and online RL, applying it to LLM training via a high-throughput inference engine like FlexGen is novel. This approach moves beyond post-hoc alignment to integrate corrective feedback directly and efficiently into the model's core generative process.

proposals.title: Inverse Concept Bottleneck Models: Controlled Generation and Interpretability via Concept-to-Input Mapping
proposals.motivation: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) have emerged as a powerful framework for interpretable AI, where a model first predicts human-interpretable concepts from an input, then uses these concepts for a final task. However, this forward mapping from input to concept is only half the story. The inverse problem—generating plausible inputs from a given concept vector—remains underexplored but is critical for model interpretability, enabling users to 'edit' predictions by manipulating concepts and to audit concept representations by visualizing their corresponding inputs. This capability would close the loop for truly interactive and debuggable concept-based systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a high-fidelity, conditional generative model can be trained to invert a frozen CBM, producing diverse and realistic inputs that faithfully correspond to a specified set of concept values, thereby enabling controlled generation and providing a direct mechanism for validating and steering concept-based reasoning.
proposals.method: We will build upon the FlexGen high-throughput inference framework to efficiently train a large conditional diffusion model or transformer decoder. This 'inverter' network will take a concept vector (e.g., from a pre-trained CBM on bird species) as conditioning and generate the corresponding input (e.g., an image). Training will use a reconstruction loss on generated inputs, paired with adversarial and concept-prediction consistency losses to ensure output realism and concept alignment. We will develop specific evaluation tasks measuring output diversity, concept fidelity (using the original CBM), and the utility of the inverter for interactive concept-based editing.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel generative framework for inverting interpretable model representations, 2) a new tool for auditing and debugging concept bottlenecks by generating counterfactual examples, and 3) a methodology for controlled generation where edits in concept space directly map to coherent changes in the input space, advancing the ecosystem of interpretable machine learning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on the forward pass of CBMs or uses concepts as weak supervision for generation, our approach directly and explicitly learns the deterministic (but one-to-many) inverse mapping from a frozen concept space to the input distribution, treating the CBM as a fixed, interpretable representation layer for a generative model.

proposals.title: Dual: Extracting Human-Understandable Concepts via Natural Language Explanations of Latent Representations
proposals.motivation: Modern large language models achieve high performance but remain black boxes, limiting trust and safe deployment. Pre-2026 interpretability work, such as concept activation vectors, often requires pre-defined concepts or yields ungrounded abstractions. This project addresses the critical need to extract inherently human-understandable concepts directly from a model's latent dynamics to improve transparency and control.
proposals.hypothesis: By training a lightweight, auxiliary explanation generator to produce natural language descriptions of a frozen base model's internal states during inference, we can distill these explanations into a discrete, sparse set of human-interpretable concepts that causally influence the model's predictions.
proposals.method: We will adapt a high-throughput inference framework like FlexGen to run a large, frozen base model (e.g., LLaMA). We introduce a small, trainable 'Dual' module that takes intermediate layer activations as input and generates a natural language explanation for the model's ongoing reasoning. Using a method inspired by the Eliciting Latent Knowledge (ELK) paradigm, we will cluster and sparsify these generated explanations across many inputs to induce a finite concept dictionary. The causal role of each concept will be validated via targeted interventions on the latent representations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel method for post-hoc concept extraction that is grounded in a model's own explanatory language. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating the fidelity of extracted concepts to model internals, advancing the field of mechanistic interpretability beyond probing and saliency maps.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior concept-based methods, Dual learns concepts *from* the model's self-generated explanations rather than mapping latents to pre-defined human concepts, enabling the discovery of novel, model-internal abstractions. It uniquely combines high-throughput inference with explanation generation for scalable concept induction.

proposals.title: ParaScore: A High-Throughput, Multi-Granularity Framework for Robust Paraphrase Detection in LLM Watermarking
proposals.motivation: Current distortion-free watermarking schemes for language models, like those in Liang's recent work, often rely on simple hash-based or n-gram paraphrase scoring. These methods can be brittle to minor syntactic rephrasing, limiting robustness and enabling evasion. Incorporating a more sophisticated, semantically-aware paraphrase model is critical for creating watermarks that are both distortion-free and resilient to practical adversarial rewriting.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a high-throughput, inference-optimized neural paraphrase model into the watermarking generation and detection pipeline will significantly improve robustness against semantic-preserving text transformations without compromising the watermark's statistical guarantees or generation speed.
proposals.method: We will adapt the FlexGen high-throughput inference framework to efficiently run a contrastively-trained sentence transformer (e.g., SBERT) or a lightweight encoder-decoder model (e.g., T5-small) for real-time paraphrase scoring during the watermark's candidate token ranking. The model will be trained on diverse paraphrase corpora (e.g., PAWS, QQP) to capture lexical, syntactic, and discourse-level equivalences. We will design a hybrid scoring function that combines the neural model's semantic score with the existing hash-based rule to balance robustness and security.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, open-source watermarking system with demonstrably higher robustness to paraphrasing attacks. It will also contribute a methodological blueprint for integrating complex neural scorers into constrained, real-time inference pipelines, bridging the gap between high-accuracy NLP models and efficient systems for LLM security.
proposals.novelty: While neural paraphrase models exist, their integration into the low-latency, single-GPU inference context of distortion-free watermarking is unexplored. The novelty lies in co-designing the model architecture and the inference system (FlexGen) to meet the unique throughput and latency constraints of the watermarking task.

proposals.title: Inverse Context Generation: A Controlled Generation Framework for Diagnosing and Controlling Question Answerability
proposals.motivation: Current QA systems are evaluated on static datasets, offering limited insight into how specific context properties govern answerability. This makes it difficult to systematically diagnose model failures or construct adversarial examples. A method to generate contexts that precisely control the answerability of given question pairs would enable targeted stress-testing and dataset augmentation for more robust models.
proposals.hypothesis: Large language models can be guided, via constrained generation and inference-time techniques, to produce minimal, fluent contexts that satisfy precise logical constraints on question answerability, revealing model biases and capabilities in context-question reasoning.
proposals.method: We will frame the task as a controlled text generation problem. Building on FlexGen's high-throughput inference, we will fine-tune a T5 or GPT-style model using a reinforcement learning objective that rewards contexts where a frozen QA model's confidence score for the 'answerable' question is high and for the 'unanswerable' counterpart is low. We will incorporate techniques from anticipatory generation to maintain coherence and explore inference-time algorithms, inspired by watermarking's logit manipulation, to enforce the answerability constraint without full fine-tuning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel diagnostic tool for QA model analysis and a method for generating challenging, controlled evaluation examples. It will also advance the field of constrained text generation by tackling a problem requiring precise logical manipulation of semantic properties within a narrative.
proposals.novelty: While prior work generates questions from contexts, we invert this pipeline to generate contexts for questions with a specific answerability outcome. This moves beyond simple keyword insertion, requiring the model to reason about the semantic and logical implications of generated text on external QA systems.

proposals.title: Efficient Personalization of On-Device Language Models via Compressed Prefix-Tuning
proposals.motivation: The deployment of small language models (e.g., <100M parameters) on edge devices is critical for privacy and latency, but these models lack the capacity for multi-task or personalized performance. Full fine-tuning is infeasible due to storage and compute constraints. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods like prefix-tuning, developed for large models, have not been systematically optimized for the unique efficiency-performance trade-offs of tiny models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a highly compressed and quantized prefix-tuning framework can enable effective, multi-task personalization for on-device models without degrading their core inference efficiency, and that this approach will outperform full fine-tuning and adapter-based methods in a per-parameter utility metric.
proposals.method: We will adapt the prefix-tuning algorithm (Li & Liang, 2021) for sub-100M parameter models (e.g., TinyLLAMA, DistilGPT-2). The core technical innovation is the integration of aggressive compression: we will apply low-bit quantization (e.g., 4-bit) and structured pruning to the continuous prefix vectors. We will develop a training regime using the FlexGen high-throughput inference system to simulate on-device constraints and evaluate on a curated 'ecosystem' of personalized tasks (e.g., text style adaptation, domain-specific keyword recognition).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a practical PEFT library for tiny models, enabling dynamic task switching on device. It will provide the first systematic analysis of how prefix compression interacts with model scale, establishing new benchmarks for on-device personalization. The resulting methods will advance the frontier of efficient, performant edge AI.
proposals.novelty: While prefix-tuning is known for large models, its application and optimization for the severely constrained parameter and memory budgets of tiny on-device models is unexplored. The integration of inference-aware compression directly into the PEFT training loop represents a novel synergy between efficient generation and efficient adaptation.

proposals.title: Antagonistic Scaling Laws: Task-Conditional Data Valuation for Predicting Performance Frontiers
proposals.motivation: Current scaling laws treat data as a homogeneous resource, but real-world datasets are mixtures of sources with varying quality and utility for specific tasks. This monolithic view fails to predict when adding more data from a particular source yields negative returns on target metrics like robustness or fairness, leading to inefficient and potentially harmful model training. A principled framework for task-conditional data valuation is needed to guide efficient data curation and understand the complex interactions between data composition and model behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: The scaling behavior of a model on a target task can be decomposed into a set of interacting, often antagonistic, scaling trajectories for distinct data sources or qualities, enabling the prediction of a performance frontier where further data from a source provides diminishing or negative returns.
proposals.method: We will develop a task-conditional extension of data Shapley value estimation, using efficient approximations inspired by methods like FlexGen for high-throughput evaluation. For a target task T (e.g., OOD robustness), we will train a series of models on controlled mixtures of data sources (e.g., high-quality vs. web-crawled) and fit multi-source scaling laws to model performance on T and antagonistic metrics. A core algorithmic contribution will be a procedure to infer the performance frontier—the optimal data mixture for a given compute budget—and validate it via rigorous OOD testing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical method for predicting the value of data subsets for specific model behaviors before large-scale training, enabling more efficient and targeted data collection. It will introduce the concept of antagonistic scaling laws, formally characterizing trade-offs between different data sources and offering a new lens for understanding dataset composition effects on generalization.
proposals.novelty: While prior work on data valuation (e.g., Data Shapley) assigns a static score, our framework makes valuation explicitly conditional on a target task and model behavior. Furthermore, moving beyond a single scaling curve to model interacting and antagonistic trajectories between data sources is a novel departure from existing scaling law literature.

proposals.title: The Synthetic Observatory: Using Foundation Models as Controllable Simulators for Systematic Hypothesis Testing in NLP
proposals.motivation: Current NLP research primarily treats foundation models as starting points for fine-tuning, focusing on end-task performance. This overlooks their potential as instruments for scientific discovery about learning and generalization itself. By drawing a formal analogy to astrophysical simulations, we can repurpose these models to generate controlled, massive synthetic datasets, enabling principled investigation of core questions in domain adaptation and algorithm design that are difficult to study with limited real-world data alone.
proposals.hypothesis: Foundation models, when used as configurable synthetic data generators, can serve as effective 'observatories' for testing hypotheses about domain gaps, adaptation algorithm robustness, and the latent structure of NLP tasks, with findings that meaningfully predict performance on real-world benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where a frozen large language model (LLM) is prompted to generate diverse synthetic datasets across a parameterized space of styles, constraints, and biases. Inspired by FlexGen's high-throughput inference, we will efficiently sample this space. We will then use these synthetic datasets as testbeds to: 1) Diagnose the failure modes of existing adaptation algorithms (e.g., fine-tuning, prompting) under controlled distribution shifts, and 2) Stress-test new adaptation methods by evaluating their generalization from synthetic 'training' domains to held-out real-world data, treating the real data as the final observational ground truth.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new methodological paradigm for using foundation models as scientific tools rather than just task solvers. It will yield a publicly available framework for generating hypothesis-testing suites and provide empirical insights into the relationship between synthetic data properties and adaptation algorithm generalization, advancing the theoretical understanding of transfer learning.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the conceptual shift from direct adaptation to systematic simulation and hypothesis testing, formalized through the astrophysics analogy. While synthetic data generation exists, its use for controlled, large-scale experimentation on adaptation *algorithms themselves*—rather than for training a final model—is underexplored.

proposals.title: SimuLEX: Co-Designing Foundation Models for Domain-Specific Simulation via Hybrid-Objective Pretraining
proposals.motivation: Current foundation models are pretrained on broad, generic data and later adapted for domain-specific simulation (e.g., in materials or climate science), often requiring extensive fine-tuning and generating outputs that violate domain constraints. This leads to inefficient, brittle, and uninterpretable simulators. A foundational approach that internalizes domain structure from the start is needed to produce robust, high-fidelity synthetic data for scientific and technical domains where real data is scarce.
proposals.hypothesis: A foundation model co-designed from the start with a hybrid objective—combining next-token prediction on a broad corpus with a structured, domain-specific objective (e.g., enforcing physical laws or relational constraints)—will yield a generative engine that synthesizes more principled, high-quality, and data-efficient synthetic scenarios than one created by fine-tuning a generic model.
proposals.method: We will extend the Transformer architecture with a modular, multi-objective training framework. During pretraining, each batch will mix generic web text with structured domain data (e.g., scientific papers, code, symbolic representations). The loss function will combine standard autoregressive loss with a domain-specific regularization term, such as a physics-informed neural network (PINN) loss for continuous systems or a graph consistency loss for relational data. Inspired by FlexGen's efficiency, we will develop high-throughput training strategies to manage the computational load of the hybrid objective.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new paradigm for building domain-aware foundation models, shifting from post-hoc adaptation to co-design. It will deliver a concrete methodology and open-source model for a pilot domain (e.g., molecular generation) that demonstrates superior simulation fidelity and data efficiency. The resulting 'simulator foundation model' will be a tool for scalable synthetic data generation.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that injects domain knowledge via fine-tuning or prompting, this approach architecturally fuses broad and narrow objectives at the foundational pretraining stage. It moves beyond task adaptation to create a model whose core utility is principled simulation, a departure from standard language modeling goals.

proposals.title: DynaBench: Iterative Robustness-Aware Co-Training with Dynamic Adversarial Evaluation
proposals.motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) are brittle, failing on adversarial or out-of-distribution inputs not represented in static benchmarks. Pre-training and fine-tuning are largely decoupled, with evaluation serving as a passive, final check. This creates a vulnerability cycle where models overfit to known test sets, leaving systemic weaknesses unaddressed. A training paradigm that actively uses evaluation to guide adaptation is needed for robust, reliable models.
proposals.hypothesis: An iterative co-training loop, where a dynamic adversarial benchmark identifies failure modes to generate targeted fine-tuning data, will systematically and efficiently improve model robustness beyond static training and evaluation methods.
proposals.method: We propose DynaBench, a framework with two interleaved components: a Dynamic Benchmark Generator and a Robustness-Aware Trainer. The generator, inspired by adversarial data collection methods like Dynabench, uses a suite of rule-based and model-based adversaries to probe the current model's worst-case performance, creating a 'stress test' benchmark. The trainer then uses techniques from data augmentation and targeted fine-tuning (e.g., generating synthetic examples for identified failure modes using methods akin to those in 'Anticipatory Music Transformer') to patch these vulnerabilities. This loop repeats, with the benchmark evolving to target newly hardened model surfaces. We will leverage efficient inference methods (e.g., FlexGen) to make the adversarial generation and evaluation phase computationally feasible.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a novel, automated training framework for iterative robustness improvement, 2) the DynaBench system and a resulting progressively robustified model series, and 3) empirical evidence on the efficiency and limits of co-closing the train-test distribution gap. It moves robustness from a static evaluation goal to an integrated training objective.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on static adversarial training or one-off data augmentation, DynaBench formalizes a closed-loop, iterative arms race between model and evaluator. It fundamentally merges the benchmark development cycle (typically human-driven) with the model training cycle, creating a self-improving system for robustness.

proposals.title: TaskTransfer: Predicting Pre-training Utility via Distribution Alignment of Solution and Target Manifolds
proposals.motivation: Current self-supervised pre-training for NLP relies on a small set of generic tasks (e.g., masked language modeling), selected heuristically. This is inefficient and suboptimal for specialized downstream domains, where a chosen pre-training task can be neutral or even harmful. A principled, predictive framework for task selection is needed to move beyond trial-and-error and enable efficient, targeted model development.
proposals.hypothesis: The transfer performance of a self-supervised pre-training task for a specific target domain can be reliably predicted by measuring the divergence between the data distribution induced by the task's solution manifold and the target domain's data distribution.
proposals.method: We will formalize the 'solution manifold' of a pre-training task (e.g., BERT's MLM, GPT's causal LM, Anticipatory Music Transformer's future token prediction) by analyzing the feature space of models trained to solve it. Using tools from the 'ecosystem' of model analysis, we will compute distributional divergences (e.g., Wasserstein, MMD) between these manifolds and target domain data. We will then train a meta-predictor on a suite of diverse (task, target) pairs to estimate downstream performance from these divergences and small-scale probe network results, validating predictions via controlled fine-tuning experiments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a theoretical framework linking pre-training task structure to downstream utility, 2) a practical diagnostic suite for estimating transfer performance without full-scale training, and 3) empirical guidelines for selecting or designing pre-training tasks tailored to known target domains, improving data and compute efficiency.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that ranks tasks universally, we model transfer as a relational property between task-induced and target distributions. This moves beyond correlation to a causal-mechanistic explanation of why certain pre-training tasks transfer.

proposals.title: GraphBERT: A Masked Node and Edge Modeling Framework for General-Purpose Graph Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Pre-training via masked language modeling (MLM) has revolutionized NLP, enabling models like BERT to learn rich, transferable representations from unlabeled text. In contrast, graph representation learning often relies on supervised or task-specific objectives, lacking a unified, self-supervised pre-training paradigm that can capture complex structural and attribute semantics across diverse graph domains (e.g., social networks, molecules, knowledge graphs). Establishing such a paradigm is crucial for building foundational models for graph-structured data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting the BERT-style masked prediction objective to graph nodes and edges—by masking node attributes, edge types, or entire subgraphs and training a model to reconstruct them from their context—will yield graph encoders that learn transferable, task-agnostic representations, significantly boosting performance on downstream tasks with limited labels.
proposals.method: We will design a Transformer-based encoder that operates on sequences of graph tokens, representing nodes and edges via linearized walks or subgraph sampling. The core pre-training task will be Masked Graph Modeling (MGM): randomly masking a portion of node feature vectors, edge types, or small sub-structures and training the model to predict them. We will explore masking strategies inspired by Percy Liang's work on 'flexible generation' and efficient inference (e.g., FlexGen) to handle the variable, non-sequential nature of graphs. The model will be evaluated by fine-tuning on benchmark tasks like node classification, link prediction, and graph classification across multiple domains.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, general-purpose self-supervised pre-training framework for graphs, analogous to BERT for language; 2) a systematic analysis of masking strategies for graph elements; and 3) open-sourced, pre-trained graph encoders that demonstrate strong few-shot and transfer learning capabilities, advancing the field towards foundational models for graph-structured data.
proposals.novelty: While MLM is standard in NLP, its principled adaptation to the non-sequential, heterogeneous structure of graphs is non-trivial and underexplored. Our approach uniquely integrates masking over both node attributes and edge connectivity within a single, flexible pre-training objective, moving beyond prior graph self-supervision methods like contrastive learning or autoencoding.

proposals.title: FlexGen-Task: High-Throughput Few-Shot Adaptation of Pre-Trained Graph Neural Networks via Meta-Learning
proposals.motivation: Pre-trained Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have become foundational for graph-structured data, but adapting them to new, low-data downstream tasks remains inefficient and computationally expensive. This bottleneck limits their application in dynamic, data-scarce domains like molecular property prediction or new social network analysis. A method for rapid, high-throughput adaptation would significantly broaden the practical utility and deployment of GNNs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a meta-learning framework, inspired by model-agnostic meta-learning (MAML) but optimized for high-throughput inference, can enable a single pre-trained GNN to quickly adapt to diverse new graph tasks with only a few gradient steps and examples per task.
proposals.method: We will develop FlexGen-Task, extending the high-throughput inference principles of FlexGen to the meta-training and adaptation loop. A large, pre-trained GNN backbone (e.g., on the Open Graph Benchmark) will be meta-trained across a distribution of tasks (e.g., node classification, link prediction, graph classification). The core innovation is a task-aware, lightweight adapter module that is rapidly optimized per task. The system will use a first-order MAML approximation for efficiency and integrate a task-embedding network to condition the adapter, enabling fast inference on novel tasks with minimal per-task parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, efficient framework for few-shot graph task adaptation, 2) a demonstration of applying high-throughput inference paradigms (FlexGen) to the meta-learning setting, and 3) a comprehensive benchmark for few-shot GNN adaptation across multiple graph domains, advancing the ecosystem of reusable graph models.
