proposals.novelty: While meta-learning for graphs exists, coupling it explicitly with high-throughput inference systems for rapid, scalable adaptation of a single pre-trained model is novel. The approach uniquely focuses on the deployment efficiency of the adaptation process itself, not just final accuracy.

proposals.title: Inverse Pre-training: Leveraging Language Model Representations for Conditional Graph Generation
proposals.motivation: Generative models for graphs, crucial for drug discovery and material science, often struggle with data efficiency and conditional generation based on complex, high-level specifications. Meanwhile, large language models (LLMs) pre-trained on vast text corpora have developed rich internal representations of entities, relationships, and abstract concepts. This project investigates whether these learned linguistic representations can be effectively repurposed to guide and improve graph generation, bridging a gap between unstructured text understanding and structured data synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the latent representations from a frozen, pre-trained language model (e.g., LLaMA or GPT-2) can serve as a powerful, semantically grounded prior to condition and improve the sample efficiency and controllability of autoregressive or diffusion-based graph generative models.
proposals.method: We will develop a model where a graph generator (e.g., a Graph Transformer or a diffusion model over adjacency matrices) is conditioned on embeddings extracted from a frozen LLM. For a given text prompt (e.g., 'a molecule soluble in water'), we will use the LLM's final-layer [CLS] token or a learned weighted sum of token embeddings as a conditioning vector. This vector will modulate the generation process via cross-attention in the graph decoder. We will adapt high-throughput inference techniques from FlexGen to manage the computational load of the LLM forward pass during training and generation, focusing on molecular graph datasets like ZINC and conditional tasks from the GuacaMol benchmark.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel method for data-efficient and controllable graph generation by leveraging pre-existing linguistic knowledge. It will provide empirical evidence on the transferability of LLM representations to non-linguistic, structured domains, and release a framework for 'inverse' use of foundational models, where representations guide the synthesis of data rather than analyze it.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses LLMs to *generate* graph descriptions or SMILES strings, our approach is novel in directly using the LLM's internal *representations* as a conditioning signal for a dedicated graph-structured generator, inverting the typical analysis-for-generation pipeline. This differs from joint multimodal pre-training and is unexplored for explicit graph topology generation.

proposals.title: Evaluating Robust Distortion-Free Watermarks for Language Models on Large-Scale, Diverse QA Ecosystems
proposals.motivation: The deployment of large language models (LLMs) in real-world applications necessitates reliable mechanisms to distinguish AI-generated text from human-written content, especially for high-stakes domains like education and fact-checking. While recent work like Liang's Robust Distortion-free Watermarks offers a promising, zero-cost solution, its evaluation has been limited to relatively narrow benchmarks. To assess true robustness and utility, the method must be tested on the scale and diversity of real-world question-answering data, where linguistic style, domain complexity, and answer length vary dramatically.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the statistical robustness and perceptual quality of distortion-free watermarking schemes degrade predictably with increased dataset scale and linguistic diversity, revealing failure modes not apparent on smaller, curated benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will apply the Robust Distortion-free Watermarking algorithm, which operates by biasing token selection during generation via a pseudorandom function keyed to prior context, to a suite of large-scale QA datasets (e.g., Natural Questions, TriviaQA, domain-specific subsets from the Data Ecosystem). Using the high-throughput inference techniques from FlexGen, we will generate watermarked answers at scale. Evaluation will measure watermark detection rates (TPR/FPR), text quality metrics (e.g., MAUVE, perplexity), and robustness against paraphrasing attacks across different dataset domains and sizes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale empirical analysis of a distortion-free watermark's performance across a realistic data ecosystem, establishing concrete bounds on its practical utility. It will yield a public benchmark for future watermarking research and inform best practices for deploying such techniques in diverse applications.
proposals.novelty: While prior work validates watermarks on controlled text, this systematically evaluates their scaling behavior and domain sensitivity on massive, heterogeneous QA pairs—a critical step towards real-world deployment that remains unexplored.

proposals.title: FlexGen-Incremental: High-Throughput, Low-Latency Incremental Parsing for Interactive Dialogue
proposals.motivation: Interactive dialogue systems require real-time understanding of user utterances, but standard parsers operate on complete sentences, introducing latency and breaking conversational flow. Pre-2026 work on incremental processing in NLP is limited and often not optimized for the high-throughput, low-latency demands of modern generative dialogue agents. This gap hinders the development of truly responsive and natural conversational AI.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting the high-throughput inference techniques from FlexGen and the anticipatory generation principles from the Anticipatory Music Transformer, we can design a neural parser that performs accurate, real-time syntactic and semantic analysis incrementally as words are spoken, with minimal computational overhead.
proposals.method: We will adapt a transformer-based parser (e.g., a dependency or constituency parser) to process input in a streaming, word-by-word fashion. The core architecture will integrate a state management system inspired by FlexGen's offloading strategies to maintain hidden states efficiently, and an anticipation mechanism akin to the Anticipatory Music Transformer to predict plausible upcoming structures. Training will use a curriculum on dialogue corpora, with a loss function that rewards early and accurate partial parse decisions. Evaluation will measure both parsing accuracy (F1) and critical latency metrics (time-to-first-parse, throughput).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel parsing framework that enables real-time, incremental understanding in dialogue agents, directly reducing perceived latency. It will contribute a new benchmark for evaluating incremental NLP models and demonstrate how high-throughput inference techniques can be repurposed for low-latency structured prediction tasks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of high-throughput LLM inference methods (FlexGen) with anticipatory modeling for a core NLP task (parsing), specifically optimized for the interactive dialogue domain, moving beyond batch-oriented parsing paradigms.

proposals.title: Scaling Robust Self-Training to Billion-Scale Unlabeled Corpora: A Study on ImageNet and Beyond
proposals.motivation: Self-training has shown promise in semi-supervised learning, but its robustness and scalability to truly massive, noisy, and diverse unlabeled datasets remain underexplored. As models and datasets grow, understanding the practical limits and failure modes of self-training on web-scale corpora is crucial for reducing reliance on expensive human annotation. This work directly addresses the challenge of leveraging the vast, unlabeled data available in domains like vision, where methods like FixMatch have succeeded on smaller scales.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a carefully engineered robust self-training pipeline, incorporating consistency regularization and confidence-based filtering, can effectively scale to billion-image corpora like ImageNet-21K and YFCC100M, leading to significant performance gains over supervised baselines and smaller-scale semi-supervised methods.
proposals.method: We will adapt and scale the robust self-training framework from works like FixMatch and Noisy Student. The core method involves iteratively training a teacher model on labeled data, generating pseudo-labels on a massive unlabeled corpus (e.g., ImageNet-21K, YFCC100M), and training a student model on the combined set with strong data augmentation and confidence thresholding. We will leverage high-throughput inference techniques, inspired by FlexGen, to efficiently generate pseudo-labels at scale. The study will systematically vary dataset size, noise levels, and architectural capacity to identify scaling laws and bottlenecks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive empirical study of robust self-training at the billion-sample scale, establishing new benchmarks for semi-supervised learning on ImageNet-21K. It will yield practical insights into the computational, algorithmic, and data-centric challenges of scaling self-supervision, informing future research on learning from web-scale data.
proposals.novelty: While self-training is known, its systematic application and analysis at the scale of billion-image datasets, with a focus on robustness and throughput constraints, is novel. The integration of high-efficiency inference strategies to manage the computational cost of pseudo-labeling at this scale represents a significant methodological advance over prior art.

proposals.title: AlpacaFarm for the Masses: Efficient Preference Optimization for Sub-10B Parameter Models
proposals.motivation: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is critical for aligning language models with human preferences, but its high computational cost has largely restricted it to models with tens or hundreds of billions of parameters. This creates a significant accessibility gap, preventing the development of safe, performant, and specialized small models for research and practical deployment. Efficient RLHF for sub-10B parameter models would democratize alignment and enable a new ecosystem of efficient, task-specific agents.
proposals.hypothesis: By leveraging the AlpacaFarm simulator for low-cost, high-throughput preference data generation and co-designing lightweight RL optimization algorithms with inference-efficient model architectures, we can develop RLHF pipelines that significantly improve the helpfulness and harmlessness of small models without prohibitive compute.
proposals.method: We will use the AlpacaFarm ecosystem to simulate human preferences at scale for training and evaluation. Building on recent methods like Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), we will develop and benchmark memory-efficient variants suitable for limited GPU memory. We will integrate these with high-throughput inference techniques, inspired by FlexGen, to manage the computational load of sampling from multiple model copies (policy, reference, reward) during training. The study will systematically evaluate pipelines across model scales (1B, 3B, 7B parameters) on established dialogue and instruction-following tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a publicly available, reproducible pipeline for aligning small language models with human preferences, along with benchmark results on the cost-quality trade-offs of different methods. It will provide concrete guidelines for practitioners and expand the research frontier in efficient alignment beyond giant models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses AlpacaFarm for large models or focuses on inference efficiency, this proposal uniquely combines the simulator with algorithmic and systems co-design specifically to overcome the distinct bottlenecks of RLHF for sub-10B parameter models, an underexplored but critical regime.

proposals.title: The Dynamics of Adaptation: Quantifying the Gap Between Static Benchmarks and Interactive Model Performance
proposals.motivation: Current evaluation of large language models relies heavily on static benchmarks (e.g., MMLU, BIG-bench), which provide a snapshot of capability. However, real-world deployment involves continuous user interaction, where models can adapt and learn from feedback, a dynamic process not captured by static evaluation. This creates a critical gap in our understanding of model performance and safety in production environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model's performance trajectory under iterative user interaction diverges significantly from its static benchmark scores, and that this divergence can be systematically predicted by analyzing the model's internal representations and its behavior on a novel suite of dynamic inference tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a controlled interactive ecosystem where a model (e.g., LLaMA or T5) engages in multi-turn tasks with simulated users, generating and refining responses. We will instrument the model using FlexGen-style high-throughput inference to log internal states (activations, attention patterns). A key technical component will be the design of 'anticipatory' inference tasks, inspired by the Anticipatory Music Transformer, which require the model to predict and adapt to simulated user preferences over time. We will then train a diagnostic meta-model to predict dynamic performance shifts from static benchmark results and logged internal states.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) a new framework and metrics for evaluating model adaptation in interactive settings, 2) a diagnostic tool to predict dynamic performance from static evaluations, and 3) a public dataset of model interaction trajectories. This will provide a more nuanced understanding of model capabilities and inform the design of more robust evaluation paradigms.
proposals.novelty: While prior work studies adaptation via fine-tuning, we focus on in-context learning and behavioral shifts during a single interactive session, linking these dynamics to static benchmarks through model internals—a connection largely unexplored pre-2026.

proposals.title: Holistic Evaluation of Multimodal Models: A Standardized Framework for Text-Image Tasks
proposals.motivation: The rapid development of large multimodal models (LMMs) like Flamingo and GPT-4V has outpaced the creation of robust, standardized evaluation frameworks. Current benchmarks are often narrow, focusing on specific tasks like VQA or captioning, and fail to capture holistic model performance across dimensions such as robustness, fairness, and efficiency. This lack of a unified evaluation ecosystem hinders meaningful comparison and scientific progress in the field.
proposals.hypothesis: A holistic, multi-dimensional evaluation framework, extending principles from text-only models to the multimodal domain, will reveal critical performance trade-offs and failure modes in LMMs that are not apparent in current task-specific benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will extend the 'Holistic Evaluation of Language Models' (HELM) framework to multimodal inputs. This involves: 1) Curating a diverse set of text-image scenarios spanning generation, inference, and reasoning tasks, 2) Defining evaluation axes including accuracy, robustness to distribution shifts (e.g., typographic attacks, spatial reasoning), calibration, bias, and inference efficiency, and 3) Implementing a high-throughput evaluation system using FlexGen-inspired techniques to efficiently benchmark multiple LMMs across this expanded scenario-axes matrix.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a comprehensive, open-source benchmark and toolkit for standardized multimodal evaluation. It will produce a detailed performance landscape of leading LMMs, identifying their strengths, weaknesses, and the computational trade-offs involved, thereby guiding future model development and deployment.
proposals.novelty: While HELM exists for language models, its principled extension to the multimodal domain is novel. The framework uniquely integrates efficiency metrics (inspired by FlexGen) with robustness and fairness audits specifically designed for the interplay of visual and textual modalities.

proposals.title: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models at the Edge: A HELM Benchmark for Sub-100M Parameter Models
proposals.motivation: The deployment of language models on edge and mobile devices is accelerating, driven by needs for privacy, latency, and connectivity. However, the dominant evaluation paradigm, exemplified by the Holistic Evaluation of Language Models (HELM), focuses on large-scale models, leaving a critical gap in understanding the true capabilities and failure modes of tiny models (<100M parameters) in real-world scenarios. A systematic, multi-dimensional benchmark is needed to guide both model design for resource-constrained environments and informed deployment decisions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying the HELM framework to tiny language models will reveal non-linear scaling laws and specific, high-stakes domains where small models can perform adequately, and that their performance profiles differ fundamentally from larger models, being more sensitive to domain shifts and inference-time optimizations.
proposals.method: We will adapt the HELM framework to target models under 100M parameters (e.g., distilled versions of T5, GPT-2, or custom architectures). The core technical approach involves: 1) Curating a representative subset of HELM scenarios and metrics prioritized for edge use cases (e.g., dialogue, classification, summarization). 2) Implementing efficient evaluation using inference optimization techniques inspired by FlexGen to run benchmarks on single, low-resource GPUs or CPUs. 3) Conducting a granular analysis of performance across accuracy, efficiency (latency, memory, energy), and robustness axes, correlating results with architectural choices and distillation strategies.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first publicly available, standardized benchmark suite for holistic evaluation of tiny language models, filling a major gap in the ecosystem. It will provide actionable insights for model developers targeting edge devices and establish a foundation for future research into efficient model architectures and training techniques validated against real-world deployment criteria.
proposals.novelty: While HELM evaluates large models and others benchmark mobile ML tasks, no work has systematically applied a holistic, multi-metric evaluation framework—spanning accuracy, robustness, fairness, and efficiency—exclusively to the emerging class of sub-100M parameter language models for edge deployment.

proposals.title: Label-Guided Data Corruption: Using Small Labeled Sets to Structure Self-Supervision on Large Unlabeled Corpora
proposals.motivation: Semi-supervised learning traditionally uses a small labeled dataset to guide learning from a large unlabeled corpus, often through consistency regularization. However, the design of the corruption function for generating augmented views (e.g., token masking, dropout) is typically generic and task-agnostic. This work posits that the valuable signal in labeled data is not just for direct supervision, but for learning how to corrupt unlabeled data in a semantically meaningful way that preserves task-relevant structure, thereby improving the efficiency and specificity of self-supervised regularization.
proposals.hypothesis: A corruption model, parameterized and trained on a small labeled dataset to generate challenging yet valid augmentations that preserve label semantics, can provide more effective regularization for self-supervised learning on a primary unlabeled corpus than standard, task-agnostic corruption functions.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage framework. First, on the labeled set, we train a conditional generative model (e.g., a lightweight Anticipatory Music Transformer-style decoder) to produce corrupted versions of inputs where the original label remains predictable. This 'label-preserving corruption model' learns task-specific transformations. Second, we apply this learned corruption model to the large unlabeled dataset to generate paired views for a consistency regularization loss (e.g., SimCSE, FixMatch) when training the primary model. We will adapt high-throughput inference techniques from FlexGen to make the corruption generation efficient at scale.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel method for injecting weak supervision into the data augmentation process itself, bridging the gap between heuristic augmentation and learned, task-aware data synthesis. It contributes a new perspective on the role of labeled data in semi-supervised learning and could lead to more sample-efficient use of small labeled sets, particularly in specialized domains where generic augmentations fail.
proposals.novelty: Instead of using labeled data solely for supervised loss or pseudo-labeling, we invert its role to *train the data corruption process*. This makes the regularization signal on unlabeled data directly informed by the task-specific manifold learned from labeled examples, which is distinct from prior work on automated augmentation or standard consistency training.

proposals.title: Robust In-Context Learning: Mitigating Noisy and Adversarial Demonstrations
proposals.motivation: In-context learning (ICL) assumes clean, correct demonstrations, a fragile assumption for real-world deployment where data can be noisy or adversarially manipulated. This brittleness limits the reliability and security of large language models (LLMs) in open-ended applications. Addressing this is crucial for moving ICL from a controlled benchmark setting to robust, practical use.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that LLMs can be made robust to noisy and adversarial in-context examples through a combination of inference-time filtering mechanisms and training-time objectives that teach the model to identify and discount unreliable demonstrations.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-pronged approach. First, we will adapt and extend inference-time methods like FlexGen to include a lightweight, parallel scoring module that estimates the reliability of each in-context example, potentially using consistency checks or small verification models. Second, we will create a training 'ecosystem' of tasks with procedurally generated noisy and adversarial demonstrations, using techniques from data augmentation and adversarial training to fine-tune models (e.g., LLaMA, T5) to be robust. The model will learn to attend to reliable signals while ignoring misleading ones.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a framework and benchmark for evaluating ICL robustness, moving beyond accuracy on clean data. It will deliver concrete methods to harden LLMs against corrupted context, enhancing their safety and reliability for real-world inference scenarios where perfect data curation is impossible.
proposals.novelty: While prior work studies adversarial inputs or data noise in isolation, we specifically target the in-context learning paradigm, where the 'noise' is intermingled with the task specification itself. Our integrated training-and-inference approach to context sanitization is a novel direction for robust ICL.

proposals.title: Stress-Testing In-Context Learning: A Systematic Framework for Quantifying Distribution Shift Robustness
proposals.motivation: In-context learning (ICL) is a powerful emergent capability of large language models (LLMs), but its performance is highly sensitive to the distribution of the prompt's demonstration examples. As of 2025, the field lacks a systematic understanding of the precise boundaries of ICL's robustness to distribution shifts, which is critical for reliable real-world deployment where test-time inputs often deviate from training or fine-tuning data. This work aims to rigorously characterize these failure modes to inform more robust model design and evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ICL robustness degrades predictably along quantifiable axes of distribution shift (e.g., lexical, syntactic, semantic, and task-schema shifts), and that this degradation is not uniform across model scales or architectures, revealing fundamental limits of the pretraining-then-prompting paradigm.
proposals.method: We will develop a controlled, multi-axis evaluation framework, generating synthetic and naturalistic distribution shifts across diverse NLP tasks. Building on the high-throughput inference methods from FlexGen, we will efficiently run large-scale experiments across model families (e.g., GPT, LLaMA, T5) and scales. We will analyze performance degradation curves, measure the interaction between shift type and demonstration quality, and investigate if inference-time interventions like adaptive demonstration retrieval or task calibration can mitigate these failures.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) a benchmark suite for stress-testing ICL robustness, 2) a taxonomy of distribution shift failures with quantitative boundaries, and 3) empirical insights into which model properties (scale, pretraining data, architecture) confer greater robustness. These contributions will provide a foundation for building more reliable and predictable in-context learners.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has noted ICL's sensitivity to demonstrations, our approach is novel in its systematic, multi-dimensional quantification of robustness limits, moving beyond anecdotal observation to a predictive, axis-by-axis failure model. This connects the empirical study of ICL to core machine learning theory on distribution shift.

proposals.title: Scaling Laws for In-Context Learning: Emergence, Efficiency, and Limits at Trillion-Parameter Scale
proposals.motivation: In-context learning (ICL) is a defining emergent ability of large language models (LLMs), enabling adaptation without weight updates. However, its scaling behavior beyond current model sizes (e.g., hundreds of billions of parameters) is poorly understood. This research is critical for predicting the future capabilities and resource requirements of next-generation models, informing decisions about architectural choices and training investments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ICL performance follows a predictable, task-dependent scaling law with model size, but that the sample efficiency of ICL (i.e., the number of in-context examples required) improves super-linearly, leading to qualitative leaps in few-shot generalization for complex, compositional tasks at trillion-parameter scales.
proposals.method: We will leverage and extend the FlexGen high-throughput inference framework to conduct a massive empirical study, performing controlled ICL evaluations across a suite of tasks from the HELM ecosystem. We will analyze ICL performance trajectories using existing and newly trained model families (e.g., scaled variants of LLaMA, GPT architectures) up to the largest publicly available pre-2026 scales, and use extrapolation techniques to model performance at hypothetical trillion-parameter scales. We will specifically measure how scaling affects sensitivity to example ordering, label correctness, and the inference of latent task structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive scaling laws for in-context learning, quantifying its emergent benefits and practical limits. It will provide a predictive framework for the community and offer concrete guidance on whether scaling model parameters is the most efficient path toward advanced meta-learning capabilities compared to alternative approaches like fine-tuning.
proposals.novelty: While scaling laws for pre-training loss are established, a systematic investigation into the scaling of *in-context learning performance* across diverse tasks and at extreme model sizes is novel. This connects the scaling paradigm directly to the most promising inference-time adaptation method, moving beyond simple loss prediction.

proposals.title: DUAL: Learning to Infer In-Context Examples from Query-Output Pairs
proposals.motivation: In-context learning (ICL) is a powerful emergent capability of large language models, but its performance is highly sensitive to the choice of examples provided in the prompt. Current methods for selecting examples rely on heuristics or expensive retrieval, which do not directly optimize for the model's internal reasoning process. This work addresses the inverse problem: given a query and its desired output, can we predict the most effective in-context examples that would lead a model to produce that output?
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model can be trained to predict plausible and effective in-context examples from (query, output) pairs, and that this 'dual' mapping reveals structured, transferable knowledge about a model's internal inference process.
proposals.method: We will train a separate 'Dual' model (a decoder-only transformer) on a dataset of (query, output, in-context examples) triplets, where the input is the concatenated query and output, and the training target is the sequence of in-context examples. We will generate training data by sampling from a large base LLM (e.g., LLaMA) using methods inspired by FlexGen for high-throughput inference. The model will be evaluated on its ability to generate examples that, when used for ICL with the base model, improve task performance on held-out tasks compared to random or retrieved examples.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for understanding and automating in-context learning. It will provide a method for efficient, learned prompt construction and offer insights into the latent relationships between tasks, examples, and model predictions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work retrieves examples or generates prompts, directly learning to generate in-context examples from outputs inverts the standard ICL direction. This is a distinct, model-centric approach to prompt optimization, contrasting with task-similarity or gradient-based methods.

proposals.title: Generating Linguistically Diverse Adversarial Distractors via Controlled Text Generation and Inference-Time Perturbation
proposals.motivation: Adversarial sentence generation is crucial for robustly evaluating and improving NLP models, particularly in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and question-answering systems where distracting information can degrade performance. Current methods often produce simplistic or semantically shallow distractors, failing to test a model's deeper linguistic reasoning and robustness to plausible but incorrect information. Developing more sophisticated, linguistically diverse adversarial sentences will create better stress tests for language models and retrieval systems.
proposals.hypothesis: By combining controlled text generation techniques with inference-time perturbation strategies, we can generate adversarial distracting sentences that are more linguistically diverse and semantically plausible than those from current methods, leading to a more challenging and informative evaluation of model robustness.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, we will use a fine-tuned or prompted large language model (leveraging methods from the 'ecosystem' and 'generation' themes) to generate candidate distractors with controlled linguistic attributes (e.g., syntactic complexity, discourse relations). Second, we will apply efficient inference-time perturbation algorithms (inspired by 'flexgen' and inference optimization work) to semantically valid model outputs, introducing subtle logical contradictions, presupposition failures, or factual distortions. The adversarial quality will be evaluated using a suite of victim models across defined 'tasks'.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, open-source framework for generating high-quality adversarial text, advancing the field of NLP robustness evaluation. It will yield new insights into the specific linguistic phenomena that most effectively fool state-of-the-art models, contributing to both offensive adversarial generation and defensive model hardening.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work focused on word-level perturbations or simple paraphrasing, our approach systematically controls for high-level linguistic diversity and uses efficient inference-time methods to craft semantically coherent yet adversarially potent sentences, creating a more realistic adversarial 'ecosystem'.

proposals.title: Adversarial Multi-Hop Reasoning Traps: Generation and Evaluation for Robust Language Model Inference
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) often fail on tasks requiring multi-step reasoning, producing plausible but incorrect chains of thought. Pre-2026 adversarial attacks primarily target single-step classification or entailment, failing to probe the complex inference processes central to modern LLM capabilities. This work addresses the critical need to evaluate and improve the robustness of reasoning, not just surface-level prediction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that LLMs are systematically vulnerable to adversarial examples that induce compelling but erroneous multi-step reasoning chains, and that generating such examples requires modeling the inference process itself as an adversarial target.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage adversarial generation framework. First, we will use a generator model (e.g., a fine-tuned Anticipatory Music Transformer variant) to produce candidate sentences that imply a multi-hop reasoning problem. Second, we will employ a discriminator, guided by a symbolic reasoner or a high-fidelity small model, to filter candidates where the most likely reasoning path is incorrect but superficially convincing. The generation will be optimized using techniques from FlexGen for efficient, high-throughput sampling of adversarial candidates, creating a scalable adversarial 'ecosystem' for reasoning evaluation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel dataset of adversarial multi-hop reasoning traps, 2) a new evaluation benchmark for reasoning robustness in LLMs, and 3) insights into failure modes of chain-of-thought reasoning, guiding the development of more reliable inference methods.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior adversarial attacks on static outputs, this work directly targets the dynamic, multi-step inference process. It combines high-throughput generation with a reasoning-path discriminator, creating a closed-loop system for finding failures in logical coherence rather than label flips.

ideas.title: Predictive Task Transfer: A Framework for Estimating Pre-training Utility from Source-Target Distribution Alignment
ideas.description: This research develops a theoretical and empirical framework to predict whether a self-supervised pre-training task will be beneficial, neutral, or harmful for a specific downstream target domain. Instead of seeking universal task rankings, we model the transfer relationship through measurable divergences between the data distributions induced by the pre-training task's solution manifold and the target domain's data manifold. The core output is a diagnostic suite and predictive model that can estimate transfer performance from small-scale probes, enabling efficient, principled selection of pre-training tasks tailored to a known target.

ideas.title: Iterative Robustness-Aware Co-Training with Dynamic Benchmarking
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel co-training paradigm where the pre-training and fine-tuning phases are not static but interact through a dynamic, adversarial evaluation benchmark. The system alternates between: 1) using a comprehensive robustness benchmark to identify the model's most critical failure modes after a pre-training phase, and 2) generating targeted fine-tuning data or objectives specifically designed to repair those identified weaknesses. This creates a feedback loop where the evaluation suite itself evolves to probe newly emergent vulnerabilities after each robustness patch, preventing overfitting to a static test set. The result is a training pipeline that systematically hones model robustness in an iterative, automated manner, fundamentally intertwining the processes of assessment and adaptation.

ideas.title: Hybrid-Objective Simulator Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research direction proposes training foundation models with hybrid objectives that combine broad, task-agnostic data with structured, domain-specific inductive biases from the very beginning of pretraining. The core goal is not to create a model for direct task adaptation, but to produce a foundational simulator or generative engine whose primary utility is to synthesize high-quality, principled synthetic data and scenarios for a target domain (e.g., materials science, climate modeling, or financial systems). By embedding the domain constraints during foundational training, the model internalizes the causal or relational rules of the domain, resulting in a simulator that is more robust, interpretable, and data-efficient than one created by fine-tuning a purely generic model. This shifts the paradigm from 'pretrain-then-adapt-for-simulation' to 'co-design-for-simulation-from-pretraining'.

ideas.title: Foundation Models as Synthetic Observatories for Hypothesis Testing
ideas.description: This idea challenges the primary assumption that foundation models are for direct adaptation. Drawing an analogy from astrophysics, where physical experiments are impossible and simulations are used as synthetic observatories to test theories against real data, we propose a parallel approach for AI. Instead of fine-tuning a foundation model for a downstream task, we would systematically use it as a configurable simulator to generate massive, diverse synthetic datasets under varied prompting 'parameters' (e.g., styles, constraints, biases). The core research activity becomes using these synthetic datasets as a controlled testbed: comparing them to real-world data to diagnose domain gaps, stress-testing the generalization of proposed adaptation algorithms, or exploring the latent space of possible task manifestations. The solution is adapted by replacing 'cosmological parameters' with 'prompt/ generation parameters', 'telescope observations' with 'real-world datasets', and 'theory validation' with 'hypothesis testing about learning and adaptation'. The primary use path of the foundation model is thus for systematic exploration and theory-constrained data generation, not for producing a directly deployable model.

ideas.title: Task-Conditional Data Valuation and Antagonistic Scaling Laws
ideas.description: This research challenges the static notion of data quality by developing a framework where the value of a data point or subset is explicitly conditioned on the target task and desired model behavior (e.g., robustness, fairness, efficiency). It models data scaling not as a monolithic curve but as a set of interacting, sometimes antagonistic, trajectories for different data sources and qualities. The core output is a method to predict the performance frontier for a given task, identifying the optimal mixture and volume of data sources before hitting steeply diminishing or negative returns, with rigorous OOD validation to ensure discovered subsets confer genuine generalization.

ideas.title: Architecturally-Directed Data Curation for Strategic Model Diversification
ideas.description: This research investigates how different neural architectures interact with data subsets of varying quality and composition, moving beyond the assumption that all models benefit from the same massive, undifferentiated dataset. The core hypothesis is that architectural inductive biases can be strategically matched with curated, high-value data subsets to efficiently train models that develop diverse, complementary capabilities and internal representations. This approach aims to define 'architectural scaling laws' that prescribe optimal data diets for different model families, thereby mitigating homogenization by design. The outcome is a principled methodology for building a robust ecosystem of AI models, where diversity is an engineered feature achieved through co-design of architecture and data strategy.

ideas.title: Adaptive Immune System-Inspired Co-Evolution of Pre-training and Fine-Tuning
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption of a fixed, one-way pipeline from pre-training to fine-tuning by drawing an analogy from immunology. In adaptive immune systems, general immunity and specific responses co-evolve in a continuous loop. Adapting this, we propose a machine learning framework where a core 'foundational' model and peripheral 'task-adapter' modules engage in iterative, bidirectional updates. The foundational model is not frozen after initial pre-training but is refined using meta-gradients or distilled knowledge from successful (and unsuccessful) fine-tuning runs on a stream of tasks. Simultaneously, every new fine-tuning process is contextually initialized with the current foundational model, allowing task-specific learning to be more efficient and informed. This creates a dynamic system where fine-tuning outcomes directly shape future general-purpose representations, breaking the rigid sequential assumption and enabling lifelong, adaptive model improvement.

ideas.title: Apply masked language modeling to graphs via ecological network gap-filling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from ecological network gap-filling to the problem of graph pre-training. In ecology, researchers predict missing species interactions (edges) in food webs by leveraging the known network structure and node (species) traits. We adapt this by formulating a BERT-style masked prediction task for graphs: randomly mask a subset of edges (or node attribute vectors) in a graph's adjacency/feature matrix and train a graph neural network to reconstruct them. This self-supervised pre-training task, analogous to inferring an ecosystem's complete interaction web from partial data, forces the model to learn robust structural and semantic representations of nodes and edges, which can then be fine-tuned for downstream tasks like node classification or link prediction.

ideas.title: Functional Diversity Optimization via Adversarial Portfolio Search
ideas.description: This research develops a framework to systematically discover and maintain functionally diverse AI model portfolios that are robust to distributional shift and adversarial threats. It defines a formal diversity utility metric based on complementary performance across a suite of evolving, automatically generated tasks and adversarial perturbations. An optimization process, such as quality diversity search, is used to find a high-utility set of models that differ in architecture, training objective, and internal representations. The system continuously validates and updates the portfolio against novel challenges, ensuring the diversity directly counters homogenization risks by providing reliable, collective robustness.

ideas.title: Multi-Faceted Graph Reconstruction via Masked Modeling
ideas.description: This research direction adapts masked language modeling to graphs, but instead of predicting a single most-likely attribute or connection for a masked node or edge, the model is explicitly designed to produce a diverse set of plausible reconstructions. The pre-training objective trains the model to understand the multi-faceted context of a graph region, enabling it to propose several valid completions that reflect different possible relational interpretations or community structures. This approach captures the uncertainty and heterogeneity present in real-world graph data, such as social networks or knowledge graphs, where a single node can have ambiguous or multiple valid roles. The resulting model provides a richer, more nuanced foundation for downstream tasks like link prediction, node classification, and graph generation.

ideas.title: Few-Shot GNN Adaptation via Immune System-Inspired Meta-Learning
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogy from immunology to the problem of few-shot adaptation for Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). The vertebrate adaptive immune system solves the analogous problem of defending against novel pathogens with limited initial exposure by using a meta-learning-like process: it maintains a broad repertoire of generalized receptors, selects the best-matched ones upon encountering a new antigen, and then rapidly specializes them through hypermutation. Adapted to the research context, we propose a meta-learning framework where a pre-trained GNN provides a diverse "repertoire" of structural feature detectors. When presented with a new graph-based task and few labeled examples, the algorithm identifies and intensively fine-tunes only the most promising, task-relevant components of the network (e.g., specific attention mechanisms or convolutional filters). This immune system-inspired approach of selective, rapid specialization aims to achieve efficient knowledge transfer and adaptation, minimizing catastrophic forgetting and computational cost while maximizing performance on the new downstream task with limited data.

ideas.title: Portfolio Models with Epistemic Humility and Decision-Aware Output Structuring
ideas.description: This approach designs models that generate a structured portfolio of diverse, multi-faceted outputs for a single input, where each output is accompanied by explicit, quantifiable metadata. This metadata captures the model's estimated confidence, the evidential support from the input, the inferred assumptions underlying that output, and its predicted utility for different potential use-cases or loss functions. The system is trained not only to generate plausible diversity but to learn a self-assessment of when and why different facets are valid, allowing it to rank and present outputs from most to least reliable or contextually appropriate. This transforms unconstrained diversity into a actionable, decision-ready set of options with transparent reasoning.

ideas.title: Curriculum-Based Hybrid Pretraining via Multi-Objective Optimization
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from pedagogical curriculum design, which successfully blends foundational and applied learning from the start, this research challenges the assumption of purely task-agnostic pretraining. The analogous solution uses structured progressions to balance general and specific objectives. Adapted to ML, the idea is to study hybrid pretraining objectives that from the outset blend a primary task-agnostic signal (e.g., MLM) with a secondary, weighted domain-specific signal (e.g., a supervised loss on a curated dataset from the target domain). A curriculum scheduler, framed as a multi-objective optimization problem, dynamically adjusts the weighting of the domain signal based on the model's evolving performance on a held-out validation set, ensuring the model builds robust general representations while being steered early toward useful domain-specific features.

ideas.title: Orchestrated Federated Intelligence: Co-training Modular Specialists with a Learned Neural Protocol for Coherent Generalization
ideas.description: This research proposes a hybrid architecture designed to overcome the integration weaknesses of naive federated or modular systems. It co-trains an ensemble of specialized neural modules with a lightweight central orchestrator model, using a federated learning framework where all components are optimized jointly for end-to-end task performance. Critical to the design is a learned 'Neural Protocol'—a dynamic communication standard and task graph that enables the orchestrator to decompose complex queries, route subproblems, and synthesize coherent final answers, ensuring the system behaves as a unified agent. The approach is validated by benchmarking its emergent reasoning capabilities against monolithic models of equivalent total parameter count, specifically measuring performance on tasks requiring cross-domain knowledge and multi-step planning.

ideas.title: Temporal Graph Pre-training for Predictive Epidemiology of AI-Generated Misinformation
ideas.description: This research proposes designing novel self-supervised pre-training tasks for dynamic social/information graphs, where the objective is to learn representations that inherently capture the epidemiological dynamics of information spread. The core innovation is to frame temporal graph learning objectives—such as predicting future network evolution, user state transitions, or interaction patterns—explicitly through the lens of compartmental models (e.g., SIR) and their parameters. The resulting pre-trained model can then be fine-tuned to forecast the propagation trajectory and impact of emerging, AI-generated misinformation campaigns directly from early-stage network signals, providing a powerful tool for early detection and mitigation.

ideas.title: Task Toxicity Screening: Borrowing Ecological Niche Theory to Identify Harmful Self-Supervised Pre-training Tasks
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that all self-supervised pre-training tasks are universally beneficial. By drawing an analogy from ecology and toxicology, where introduced species or chemicals are not assumed to be universally good but are screened for potential harm (e.g., via hormesis models and niche competition theory), we adapt this solution to machine learning. The adapted idea is to develop a 'Task Toxicity Screening' framework. This framework would model the 'ecological niche' of a self-supervised task, defined by its interactions with specific model architectures, data domains, and downstream objectives. The goal is to create predictive criteria and assays—analogous to toxicological screening tests—that can identify tasks likely to be harmful (e.g., causing catastrophic forgetting, embedding biased representations, or optimizing for spurious features that hinder downstream adaptation) before costly full-scale pre-training is undertaken.

ideas.title: Federated Data-Centric Scaling: Optimal Modular Architectures for High-Quality, Distributed Data Regimes
ideas.description: This research investigates a combined architectural and data-scaling hypothesis: that the highest performance and efficiency frontier is defined not by a single massive model trained on all available data, but by a federated or modular system where each component is optimally scaled on a distinct, curated, high-quality data subset. It challenges both monolithic centralization and the assumption of uniform data utility by exploring scaling laws for ensembles of smaller models, each trained on specialized, premium data shards. The core novelty is determining the optimal joint configuration—how to partition data by quality and domain, and how to architect the federated system (e.g., mixture of experts, learned routing) to exploit this partition, potentially achieving superior capabilities with less total computation and without ever centralizing raw data.

ideas.title: Interpretability-Guided Hybrid Pretraining for Cognitive Alignment
ideas.description: This research direction proposes using interpretability techniques from cognitive neuroscience (e.g., representational similarity analysis, encoding models) as a diagnostic and guiding framework for hybrid pretraining objectives. Instead of blending task-agnostic and domain-specific signals heuristically, the model's internal representations are continuously probed during training to measure their alignment with known neural or cognitive representations of concepts and tasks. The hybrid objective is then dynamically tuned to encourage the emergence of these interpretable, brain-like representations while maintaining broad competence, creating foundation models whose learning trajectory and internal structure are more transparent and cognitively plausible by design.

ideas.title: A Contrastive Theory of Emergent Graph Representations
ideas.description: This research develops a theoretical framework to understand how and why emergent capabilities arise in graph foundation models trained with contrastive objectives like SimCLR. It posits that the interaction between model scale, graph augmentation strategies, and the contrastive loss landscape induces phase transitions in the model's ability to capture latent relational structures. The theory aims to predict the scaling laws and specific data regimes required for the emergence of capabilities such as zero-shot relational inference or compositional generalization over graph topology. This provides a principled guide for designing architectures and pre-training strategies to elicit targeted emergent behaviors in relational AI systems.

ideas.title: Adaptive Difficulty Sequencing via Cognitive Load Theory
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from Educational Psychology's Cognitive Load Theory (CLT), which solves the problem of optimizing human learning by sequencing instructional material from simple to complex to manage mental effort. The analogous solution involves strategies like beginning with isolated concepts and using worked examples before progressing to high-interactivity problems. This is adapted to machine learning pre-training by proposing an adaptive difficulty sequencing mechanism. The system would monitor proxy signals for the model's 'cognitive load' (e.g., gradient patterns, loss stability) and use this to dynamically schedule tasks, automatically advancing from easy to hard data distributions or task decompositions only when the model demonstrates readiness, creating a more efficient and theoretically grounded curriculum learning strategy.

ideas.title: Dynamic Multi-Resolution Pre-training for Evolving Web-Scale Graphs
ideas.description: This research direction develops a co-training framework for web-scale graphs where pre-training and fine-tuning interact dynamically across multiple structural resolutions. Instead of a fixed, one-way pipeline, the system uses performance signals from diverse downstream tasks to guide which graph regions or hierarchical levels require refined pre-training, efficiently allocating computational resources. This enables the model to adapt its foundational representations to both the evolving nature of the massive graph (e.g., new users, connections, or topics in a social network) and shifting task demands, all while maintaining scalability through selective, multi-resolution updates. The result is a continuously self-improving graph foundation model that remains aligned with both the data's temporal dynamics and application needs.

ideas.title: Dynamic, Self-Paced Curriculum Learning via Online Difficulty Measurement
ideas.description: This approach implements an adaptive curriculum where task difficulty is not pre-defined but is measured in real-time based on the model's own learning signals. A meta-controller continuously estimates the difficulty of data points or tasks for the current model state using metrics like loss variance or learning progress. It then dynamically adjusts the training data distribution, prioritizing samples within a targeted difficulty window. This creates a closed-loop, self-paced learning system that maximizes sample efficiency and robustness by automatically finding the optimal challenge level throughout pre-training.

ideas.title: Interpretable Graph Masked Modeling via Neuroscientific Representational Similarity
ideas.description: This research direction adapts masked modeling objectives (like BERT's) to graph-structured data for pre-training, but crucially uses techniques from cognitive neuroscience—such as Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA) and encoding models—to guide and interpret the learned node/edge representations. The synergy lies in using neuroscientific interpretability as an inductive bias or a validation metric during pre-training, ensuring the model develops latent representations that mirror human-like relational reasoning or brain activity patterns for structured knowledge. This allows the resulting graph foundation model to be interrogated for *why* it makes certain inferences, linking its internal states to established cognitive theories, which neither pure predictive graph ML nor post-hoc model interpretation typically achieves.

ideas.title: Meta-Learning for the Econometric Valuation of Foundation Models and Their Externalities
ideas.description: This research direction proposes using multi-task meta-learning, specifically with adaptable graph neural networks (GNNs), to build an econometric valuation model for foundation models. The meta-learner is trained on a distribution of tasks where each task involves adapting a foundation model to a specific downstream application and then measuring its resulting economic outputs and externalities (e.g., bias propagation, environmental cost, labor market effects). The key innovation is that the trained meta-model can then be quickly adapted with only a few examples to provide a tailored, data-efficient econometric assessment for a *novel* deployment scenario of a foundation model, predicting its nuanced value and negative externalities before full-scale implementation.

ideas.title: The Distributed Brain: A Neuroscience-Inspired Framework for Modular AI Ensembles
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the necessity of massive, monolithic AI models by drawing an analogy from neuroscience. The analogous problem—how biological brains achieve general intelligence without a central processor—is solved through distributed, modular specialization and efficient interconnection. We adapt this solution by proposing a neuroscience-inspired AI framework: a federated ensemble of specialized, modular models, each analogous to a brain region (e.g., one for language parsing, one for spatial reasoning, one for memory retrieval). These modules would be trained independently or in a federated manner on relevant sub-tasks/data. Crucially, they would be integrated via a learned, lightweight communication protocol that mimics neural pathways, enabling coordinated, emergent problem-solving. The goal is to match the capabilities of a large monolithic model through decentralized specialization and efficient ensemble coordination, thereby reducing computational centralization, improving efficiency, and enhancing system interpretability by design.

ideas.title: Data-Driven Latent Control for Robust Model Steering
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the intractability of directly applying classical control to large models by first learning a compressed, actionable representation of their dynamics. It employs techniques from nonlinear system identification and representation learning to construct a latent state-space model that predicts the model's behavioral trajectories. Control policies, designed using robust or adaptive control theory within this learned latent space, can then reliably steer the full model's behavior while explicitly accounting for approximation errors and uncertainties. This creates a practical, closed-loop framework for stabilizing and directing complex model dynamics without full analytical knowledge of the system.

ideas.title: Decentralized Architectural Ecosystems for Emergent Capability and Robustness
ideas.description: This research investigates how to design and coordinate decentralized, heterogeneous AI systems—such as federated ensembles of specialized models with diverse architectures and training objectives—so that their collective intelligence surpasses that of a single massive model. The core hypothesis is that strategic architectural and objective diversity within a decentralized framework can yield emergent capabilities and superior robustness to distributional shift, which are unattainable through monolithic scaling. The work will develop methods for incentivizing and managing this diversity, exploring how different components can be composed, how knowledge is shared or distilled across the ecosystem, and how the system's overall performance and adaptability are evaluated beyond aggregate accuracy.

ideas.title: Applying Nutrient Limitation Theory from Ecology to Model Data Diets
ideas.description: This research seed inverts the assumption that more data always improves models by drawing an analogy from ecology, specifically Liebig's Law of the Minimum and ecological stoichiometry. In ecology, optimal plant growth is achieved not by indiscriminately adding all nutrients, but by diagnosing and supplying the single scarcest (limiting) nutrient. The analogous solution is this targeted, diagnostic approach to resource allocation. Adapted to machine learning, the idea investigates optimal data scaling by proposing that model performance is limited by specific deficiencies in the 'data diet'—such as a lack of certain logical structures, factual domains, or linguistic styles—rather than by total data volume. The research would develop diagnostic tools to identify a model's current 'limiting data factor' and create methodologies for curating or generating highly targeted, high-quality data subsets to precisely address that deficiency, thereby establishing more efficient and effective data scaling laws based on strategic supplementation rather than bulk collection.

ideas.title: Grounding Loops for Robust Simulation: Validating and Correcting Foundation Model Generators Against Reality
ideas.description: This research proposes a paradigm where foundation models are used as primary simulators or data generators, but within a closed-loop system designed to detect and correct their reality gaps. Instead of treating the model's output as a static resource, the approach continuously gathers strategic, real-world data to test the simulator's predictions in high-uncertainty or high-impact scenarios. Discrepancies are formally analyzed to distinguish between aleatoric uncertainty and systematic simulator bias, and this analysis drives targeted updates to the foundation model or the design of robust synthetic training curricula. The result is a self-improving, grounded simulation engine whose reliability for downstream tasks is quantitatively assured and iteratively enhanced.

ideas.title: Applying Power Grid Stability Control to Large Language Model Steering
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from power grid stability control to the problem of steering very large AI models. In electrical engineering, large power grids use control theory (e.g., automatic generation control) to monitor system state variables like frequency and apply real-time corrections to maintain stability after disturbances. Analogously, we propose to treat a large model's forward pass as a dynamical system. A lightweight 'governor' controller would continuously monitor key latent state variables (e.g., measures of attention dispersion or activation energy). Using principles from optimal or robust control, this controller would compute and inject subtle, corrective guidance signals—either as additive biases to intermediate activations or as adjustments to key parameters—during inference or fine-tuning. This closed-loop approach would actively damp undesirable behavioral oscillations (like coherence collapse or harmful deviation from a prompt's intent) and steer the model's generative trajectory towards a stable, high-performance operating regime, enhancing reliability and controllability.

ideas.title: A Meta-Framework for Comparative Representational Analysis of Biological and Artificial Systems
ideas.description: This project develops a formal meta-framework for analyzing internal representations that is agnostic to the substrate (biological or artificial). It defines a common vocabulary and set of operations—such as measures of disentanglement, robustness, and causal intervention—that can be applied to both neural recordings and foundation model activations. The core methodology involves running parallel experiments on both systems using matched stimuli and tasks, then using the differences and similarities in their representational geometries to isolate the unique computational principles of large-scale artificial learning. This yields interpretability insights grounded in comparative science, not flawed analogy.

ideas.title: Econometric Evaluation of Pre-trained Graph Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research direction develops a unified framework that combines scalable graph pre-training with econometric evaluation. We create efficient algorithms to pre-train foundation models on web-scale graphs (e.g., social networks, citation networks, supply chains) and simultaneously employ econometric models—such as difference-in-differences, instrumental variables, and network spillover analysis—to quantify the causal economic value and negative externalities (e.g., inequality, misinformation diffusion, systemic risk) produced by these models. The key innovation is using the econometric measurements as an objective function to iteratively refine the graph pre-training objectives, architecture, and data sampling, steering the development of graph foundation models towards maximizing positive economic externalities and minimizing societal harms at scale.

ideas.title: Contrastive Graph Representation Learning via Ecological Niche Modeling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the contrastive framework from computational ecology, specifically species distribution modeling (e.g., MaxEnt), to graph representation learning. In ecology, a species' niche is defined by contrasting its presence locations against a background of pseudo-absence locations. Analogously, for a node in a graph, we define its positive view as an augmented version of its local structural 'niche' (e.g., a subgraph sampled via random walks). The key adaptation is the construction of negative samples: instead of only using augmented versions of the same node, we systematically sample subgraphs from disparate parts of the network (or other graphs in a multi-graph dataset) to serve as a background of 'pseudo-absence' environments. The contrastive objective (like SimCLR) is then used to train an encoder to maximize similarity between the node and its positive niche view while minimizing similarity between the node and these representative negative background contexts, thereby learning a robust representation that captures the node's unique structural and functional role within the graph ecosystem.

ideas.title: Governance-Aware Self-Supervised Learning: A Framework for Pre-Training Task Selection with Societal Impact
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel framework that integrates interdisciplinary governance models directly into the core process of self-supervised learning (SSL) task design and selection. It moves beyond purely technical criteria for task utility to develop a 'governance-aware' evaluation metric that assesses potential pre-training tasks for their propensity to encode societal biases, legal non-compliances, or ethical harms. The outcome is a set of validated, governable pre-training protocols and a corresponding governance toolkit that allows developers, auditors, and policymakers to collaboratively steer foundational model development toward societally beneficial outcomes from its earliest, most formative stage.

ideas.title: Develop a Statistical Mechanics Framework for Emergent Capabilities in Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to create theoretical frameworks for understanding emergent capabilities in foundation models by drawing an analogy to statistical mechanics. In that field, the emergent macroscopic properties of complex systems (like temperature or phase transitions) are rigorously derived from the statistical behavior of many microscopic components. The analogous solution involves defining statistical ensembles and partition functions to bridge scales. Adapted to AI, the research would develop a formalism where the model's parameters, architecture, and training data constitute the 'microscopic' system, and its measurable capabilities (e.g., multi-step reasoning, compositional generalization) are the 'macroscopic' observables. The goal is to create a theoretical framework that can predict the scaling laws and critical thresholds (akin to phase transitions) at which new capabilities emerge, moving beyond empirical observation to a principled, predictive science of emergence in AI.

ideas.title: A Multi-Scale Stability Theory for GNN Pre-Training
ideas.description: This research expands the original seed idea by drawing an analogy from ecology's scale transition theory. In ecology, the stability and persistence of a meta-population emerge from the coupling of local dynamics with regional dispersal and heterogeneity, formally preventing system-wide collapse. We adapt this solution to the problem of negative transfer in Graph Neural Network (GNN) pre-training. The adapted idea is to develop a 'Multi-Scale Stability Theory for GNN Pre-Training' that formally separates and analyzes learning dynamics at node, subgraph, and whole-graph levels. By modeling the transfer of parameters and representations across these scales as a coupled dynamical system, the research will derive formal, testable conditions—based on graph spectra, task similarity, and optimization landscapes—that predict when multi-level pre-training prevents negative transfer (achieving cross-scale stability) and leads to improved generalization on downstream tasks (emergent positive transfer).

ideas.title: Adapting Geologic Stratigraphy Analysis to Interpret Model Layer Representations
ideas.description: This idea adapts interpretative techniques from geologic stratigraphy to analyze representations in foundation models. In geology, the layered structure of rock strata is analyzed using principles like superposition, cross-cutting relationships, and fossil correlation to reconstruct historical processes and environmental conditions. Analogously, we treat the layered architecture of a neural network as a 'cognitive stratigraphy.' The adapted research involves developing methods for 'representational superposition' to trace concept evolution through layers, identifying 'cross-cutting' computational pathways (e.g., specific attention heads or residual streams) that alter representations, and defining robust 'feature fossils'—consistent, identifiable activation patterns—to correlate learned concepts across different models or training runs. This provides a novel, structural-historical framework for interpreting how and where abstract concepts form, combine, and transform within the deep layers of a foundation model, offering an alternative to neuroscience-inspired activation analysis.

ideas.title: Multi-resolution graph pre-training using adaptive mesh refinement techniques
ideas.description: This research adapts Adaptive Mesh Refinement (AMR) from Computational Fluid Dynamics to scale multi-level pre-training to massive graphs. In CFD, AMR efficiently solves large-scale partial differential equations by using fine computational grids only in complex regions (like shock waves) and coarse grids elsewhere. Adapted to graph learning, the method involves dynamically constructing a multi-resolution graph hierarchy during pre-training, where fine-grained node/edge embeddings are computed only for subgraphs identified as structurally complex or information-rich, while stable or homogeneous regions receive coarser, cheaper representations. This selective refinement, guided by online metrics of graph complexity or learning gradient signals, allows the pre-training algorithm to maintain representational quality where it matters most while achieving the computational efficiency needed for web-scale or planet-scale social networks.

ideas.title: Temporal Contrastive Learning for Dynamic Graphs via Ecological Succession Analogy
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed on self-supervised objectives for dynamic graphs by drawing an analogy from ecology, specifically the study of ecological succession. The analogous solution involves analyzing snapshots of biological communities over time to model predictable transitions in species composition and interactions. Adapted to dynamic graphs, this inspires a pre-training task based on temporal contrastive learning. The model is trained to distinguish a true future graph snapshot from a corrupted one, or to predict the likelihood of future node/edge states based on historical sequences. This objective encourages the graph encoder to learn representations that capture the underlying 'laws of motion' governing the graph's evolution, separating persistent structural motifs from transient dynamics, much as ecologists separate climax communities from successional stages.

ideas.title: Ensemble of Specialized Sub-Models with a Gating Mechanism
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of homogeneous model outputs by drawing an analogy from community ecology. In ecology, a single habitat input sustains a diverse community of specialist species through niche partitioning. Adapting this solution, the proposed model architecture consists of an ensemble of specialized neural sub-models, each acting as a 'species' adept at a specific data facet. A trainable gating network analyzes the input and determines the weighted contribution of each specialist to a composite, multi-faceted output. This design explicitly produces diverse interpretations for a single input by structural analogy to a resilient ecological community, moving beyond a single generalist model's homogeneous perspective.

ideas.title: Applying Forest Fire Spread Models to Study Misinformation Propagation
ideas.description: This research adapts modeling frameworks from wildland fire science to study the propagation of model-generated misinformation. In fire science, spread is modeled using cellular automata and percolation theory, accounting for local fuel conditions, topography, and weather to predict firefront advancement and test containment strategies like firebreaks. The adapted idea maps this to the information ecosystem: the social network is the landscape, user susceptibility and platform engagement algorithms are the 'fuel,' and collective attention or news cycles are the 'weather.' The research would develop a computational model to simulate how AI-generated misinformation ignites and spreads through clustered communities, evaluating the effectiveness of network-based interventions—analogous to firebreaks—such as strategic fact-checking nodes or algorithmic downranking in containing viral falsehoods.

ideas.title: Immunological Security Frameworks for Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from immunology to create novel security paradigms for foundation models. The core analogy draws from biological immune systems, which solved the problem of defending a complex, adaptable organism against evolving threats through a multi-layered approach: innate immunity provides immediate, generalized protection, while adaptive immunity learns and remembers specific pathogens. Adapted to foundation models, this suggests designing a security framework with two integrated components. First, an 'innate security' layer implements static, pre-configured defenses against known vulnerability patterns (e.g., prompt injection, data extraction). Second, an 'adaptive security' layer employs specialized monitoring and learning subsystems that detect novel attack patterns during model operation, analyze them, and update defensive measures—creating an 'immunological memory' for the model. This framework directly addresses vulnerabilities arising from model scale and adaptability by providing a security system that itself scales and adapts, learning from attacks in real-time while maintaining core protective functions.

ideas.title: A Cognitive-Social Susceptibility Model for the Adaptive Spread of AI-Generated Misinformation
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid modeling framework that combines core epidemiological compartments with agent-based models of individual cognition and social influence. Each agent has an internal belief state influenced by cognitive biases and the persuasive, adaptive nature of AI-generated misinformation content. The model simulates how misinformation evolves in response to community feedback and counter-messaging, predicting spread not just as infection but as a complex function of psychological susceptibility and network-driven belief reinforcement. This allows for testing targeted interventions that address specific cognitive vulnerabilities and social network structures rather than just broadcast fact-checking.

ideas.title: Mitigate AI homogenization via portfolio-based objective design
ideas.description: This research seed proposes adapting the 'portfolio effect' from conservation ecology to mitigate architectural and objective homogenization in AI systems. In ecology, maintaining a diverse portfolio of populations with different traits buffers an ecosystem against collapse. The analogous solution is to deliberately design a diverse portfolio of AI training objectives and architectural constraints, rather than allowing convergence on a single dominant benchmark. The adapted research idea involves developing methods to define, measure, and incentivize 'objective diversity,' creating frameworks that reward the development of models with complementary specializations and varied failure modes. This aims to build a more resilient AI ecosystem where diversity of design protects against widespread, correlated failures inherent in homogenized systems.

ideas.title: Applying Environmental Impact Assessment Methods to Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research adapts the Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) methodology from environmental science to the domain of foundation models. In LCA, the environmental impacts of a product are quantified by creating a comprehensive inventory of all material/energy inputs and emission outputs across its life cycle, which are then characterized into impact categories. The adapted solution involves creating a 'Computational Life Cycle Assessment' framework for foundation models. This framework would systematically inventory the inputs (e.g., GPU hours, electricity, dataset size, researcher time) and outputs (e.g., model performance metrics, CO2 emissions, generated content with potential for harm, downstream economic displacement) across stages from research and data collection to training, deployment, and inference. Econometric and other quantitative models would then be applied to this structured inventory data to characterize and monetize the direct economic value and the negative externalities (such as environmental cost, societal harm, and labor market disruption), providing a standardized basis for measuring and comparing the full impact of different AI models.
