ideas.title: A Causal and Predictive Theory of Emergence via Multi-Scale Mechanistic Experimentation
ideas.description: This research program develops a predictive theory of emergence by tightly coupling targeted experimentation with theoretical modeling. It designs a suite of controlled, scaled-down model families and training curricula to isolate and test hypothesized drivers of emergence, such as specific data structures or architectural inductive biases. Using tools from mechanistic interpretability, it seeks to trace how these drivers cause specific circuit-level adaptations that lead to emergent capabilities. The resulting framework aims to predict the type and threshold of emergence for new model scales and data regimes, moving beyond post-hoc description to actionable, causal understanding.

ideas.title: Control-Theoretic Frameworks for Steering and Predicting Emergent Model Dynamics
ideas.description: This research direction synthesizes control theory and the study of emergence to develop a dynamical systems theory for foundation model scaling. It aims to model the training process and parameter evolution as a high-dimensional control system, where scaling factors (data, compute, parameters) are control inputs. The core synergy is using this framework to derive stability criteria and phase transitions that predict *which* emergent capabilities will appear at given scales, and to design feedback mechanisms—applied during training—to guide the model's trajectory toward desired emergent properties or away from unstable, harmful ones. This transforms emergence from a mysterious observed phenomenon into a principled engineering objective.

ideas.title: Adaptive, Tiered Governance for Societal Impact
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution of Adaptive Management (AM) from the field of Ecosystem Management and Conservation Biology to the challenge of interdisciplinary governance for societal impact. The analogous problem—managing complex, uncertain natural systems with competing stakeholder interests—was addressed through structured, iterative cycles where management actions are treated as hypotheses, implemented, monitored, and then formally adjusted based on outcomes, integrating scientific, legal, and local knowledge. The adapted idea proposes to develop governance models that institutionalize this learning-by-doing approach. Specifically, it would create frameworks where deployment strategies for new technologies are formally designed as testable interventions. An interdisciplinary governance panel (technical, legal, ethical) would be tasked with defining key impact metrics, overseeing real-world monitoring, and convening at scheduled intervals to analyze the collected data and mandate evidence-based revisions to the governance rules themselves, thereby creating a dynamic, responsive, and empirically grounded governance system.

ideas.title: A Dynamic Causal and Systemic Framework for Valuing Foundation Models and Their Externalities
ideas.description: This research develops a multi-method framework to overcome the identification and measurement challenges in valuing foundation models. It employs causal inference techniques on real-world deployment data to isolate the direct economic impact of a model from confounding factors. Simultaneously, it uses system dynamics modeling to project long-term, non-linear externalities, such as labor market evolution and information ecosystem distortions. Finally, it integrates these quantitative analyses with structured deliberative methods to assign normative weights to hard-to-quantify impacts, providing a robust, multi-dimensional assessment for policymakers and developers.

ideas.title: Control-Theoretic Governance for AI Societal Dynamics
ideas.description: This research direction develops a novel governance architecture that formally integrates control theory with interdisciplinary (technical, legal, ethical) oversight for advanced AI systems. It treats the combined AI-society interaction as a complex dynamical system, where governance bodies define target 'societal states' (e.g., fairness, transparency, safety) and use control-theoretic models to analyze stability and compute necessary interventions. This enables proactive, evidence-based steering of AI development and deployment, moving governance from reactive auditing to predictive, closed-loop regulation that can dynamically adjust to unforeseen societal impacts and model behaviors.

ideas.title: Stress-Tested Governance Circuit Breakers with Pre-Commit Protocols
ideas.description: This research develops and validates executable governance protocols for high-stakes technology deployment that remain robust under decision-making failure. Instead of aiming for ideal interdisciplinary integration, it identifies critical failure modes—such as expert deadlock, regulatory paralysis, or catastrophic oversight—through extensive simulation and red-teaming. The output is a set of pre-committed 'circuit breaker' mechanisms, clear decision authorities, and verified fallback procedures that ensure safe and timely action when standard collaborative governance processes break down.
